Understanding this two-stage architecture explains why a lagging IO thread (network issue) looks different from a lagging SQL thread (CPU or disk bound on the replica) and requires different remediation. Monitoring Seconds_Behind_Master along with separate IO and SQL thread states from SHOW REPLICA STATUS gives a complete picture of replication health.

children.title: Semi-synchronous Replication
children.description: Asynchronous replication can lose committed transactions if the primary crashes before the replica receives them, which violates durability expectations for applications that rely on at-least-one-replica acknowledgment before committing. Semi-synchronous replication holds the primary commit until at least one replica acknowledges receipt of the binary log event, reducing but not eliminating the risk of data loss on failover. The performance overhead of the network round-trip for acknowledgment must be weighed against the durability guarantees your application requires.

children.title: SHOW REPLICA STATUS
children.description: SHOW REPLICA STATUS (formerly SHOW SLAVE STATUS) is the primary command for diagnosing replication health, exposing replication lag in Seconds_Behind_Master, IO and SQL thread states, last error codes and messages, and the current position in both the relay log and primary binary log. Knowing which fields matter — and which are misleading, like Seconds_Behind_Master being NULL when the IO thread is down rather than 0 — prevents misdiagnosis during incidents. Parsing this output programmatically in monitoring scripts is a foundational DBA automation task.

children.title: Replication Filters
children.description: Replication filters allow you to include or exclude specific databases or tables from replication, which is useful for replica-specific schemas, preventing test databases from replicating to production replicas, or building purpose-specific read replicas with a subset of the data. Server-level filters set in my.cnf persist across restarts, while CHANGE REPLICATION FILTER allows dynamic modification without restart in MySQL 8.0. Misunderstanding filter syntax, particularly the difference between replicate_do_db and replicate_do_table, is a common source of replication data divergence.

children.title: Bootstrapping a New Replica
children.description: Creating a new replica requires taking a consistent snapshot of the primary — typically via XtraBackup or mysqldump with --master-data — and restoring it to the replica before starting replication threads, ensuring the replica begins replication from exactly where the snapshot was taken. The alternative of using CHANGE MASTER TO with an incorrect position or GTID set leads to data divergence that may not be immediately apparent. For GTID topologies, XtraBackup conveniently records the GTID set in xtrabackup_binlog_info, simplifying the CHANGE MASTER TO command.

resources.label: Setting Up Binary Log Replication Docs

children.title: Multi-Source Replication
children.description: Multi-source replication allows a single replica to receive and apply binary log events from multiple primaries simultaneously, enabling data consolidation use cases like aggregating regional shards into a central analytics replica. Each source gets its own replication channel with independent IO and SQL threads, making channel-level SHOW REPLICA STATUS queries necessary to diagnose per-source issues. GTID-based multi-source replication avoids transaction ID conflicts between sources that would otherwise occur with position-based approaches.

mysql-dba.title: Performance Schema and Monitoring
mysql-dba.description: Use the Performance Schema and sys schema to monitor query performance, wait events, and resource usage in real time. Set up external monitoring with Prometheus and mysqld_exporter.
mysql-dba.challenge: Use the sys schema to identify the top 10 queries by total latency, then set up Prometheus and Grafana to visualize MySQL metrics in a dashboard.

children.title: Performance Schema Tables
children.description: The Performance Schema instruments MySQL internals at a low level — recording statement events, wait events, stage events, and transaction events into in-memory tables that you query with SQL. The events_statements_summary_by_digest table aggregates queries by normalized form, showing total latency, rows examined, and call count for every distinct query pattern the server has executed. Enabling only the instruments and consumers you need avoids the overhead of collecting data you will never query, keeping Performance Schema's impact on throughput acceptable.

children.title: sys Schema Views
children.description: The sys schema is a layer of readable views and stored procedures built on top of Performance Schema that makes its raw data accessible without requiring knowledge of dozens of table names and complex join syntax. Views like sys.statement_analysis, sys.io_by_thread_by_latency, and sys.user_summary provide immediately actionable insight into what queries are slow, what threads are doing I/O, and which users are consuming the most resources. Using sys schema views during on-call incidents lets you diagnose performance problems in seconds rather than minutes.

children.title: Statement Digest Analysis
children.description: Statement digests normalize queries by replacing literal values with placeholders, allowing MySQL to group the million variants of 'SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id = N' into a single fingerprint and track aggregate performance metrics across all of them. This normalization is how you identify which query patterns are responsible for the most total latency on the server, even when individual execution times vary widely. Joining digest data with wait event data shows not just which queries are slow but why they are waiting — on locks, I/O, network, or CPU.

resources.label: MySQL Query Analysis with Performance Schema

children.title: Wait Event Analysis
children.description: Wait events categorize exactly what each MySQL thread is blocked on — lock waits, I/O waits, network waits, or mutex contention — giving you a profiler-like view of where time is being spent across the entire server. Identifying that lock waits dominate the wait profile points to transaction design problems, while I/O waits suggest buffer pool undersizing or disk saturation. This analysis provides the root cause that EXPLAIN output alone cannot reveal because EXPLAIN shows plan efficiency, not resource contention.

children.title: Prometheus mysqld_exporter
children.description: The mysqld_exporter for Prometheus scrapes dozens of MySQL status and performance variables and exposes them as time-series metrics that Prometheus can store and Grafana can visualize. Having a historical record of metrics like QPS, connections, buffer pool hit ratio, and replication lag lets you correlate performance degradation with specific events — a code deployment, a traffic spike, or a batch job — which is impossible with only real-time monitoring. The exporter is a lightweight sidecar process that requires only a read-only MySQL user with a small set of PROCESS, REPLICATION CLIENT, and SELECT privileges.

children.title: Grafana Dashboards for MySQL
children.description: Grafana dashboards turn raw Prometheus time-series data into visual panels showing QPS, connections, replication lag, buffer pool usage, and slow queries in a format that is readable by anyone from developers to managers during an incident. Pre-built dashboards from Percona's Monitoring and Management (PMM) or the Grafana dashboard library provide a working baseline that you can customize for your specific alerting thresholds and business metrics. Alerting rules configured in Grafana or Prometheus AlertManager that fire before thresholds are breached — not after — turn monitoring into incident prevention.

children.title: Percona Monitoring and Management (PMM)
children.description: PMM provides a complete out-of-the-box monitoring solution for MySQL that includes a pre-configured Prometheus instance, Grafana dashboards, Query Analytics, and advisory checks — dramatically reducing the setup time compared to assembling the stack manually. Its Query Analytics view combines Performance Schema data with execution plan details and index recommendations in a single interface, making it practical for developers as well as DBAs. PMM is open source and can be self-hosted on Docker or Kubernetes, making it accessible for teams without access to commercial monitoring products.

children.title: INFORMATION_SCHEMA for Runtime Stats
children.description: INFORMATION_SCHEMA tables provide server-level metadata about databases, tables, columns, indexes, partitions, and processlist at runtime, making them essential for building automation scripts that adapt to the current schema state rather than hard-coding table names. The PROCESSLIST and INNODB_TRX tables in INFORMATION_SCHEMA (and their more detailed equivalents in Performance Schema) show running queries and active transactions, enabling identification of blocking transactions and long-running operations during incidents. These views are the foundation of custom health check scripts and schema documentation generators.

mysql-dba.title: MySQL Configuration Tuning
mysql-dba.description: Tune MySQL configuration for production workloads by adjusting memory, I/O, and connection settings. Understand how to benchmark changes using sysbench.
mysql-dba.challenge: Run a sysbench OLTP benchmark before and after tuning InnoDB buffer pool and log file size, and document the TPS improvement.

children.title: innodb_buffer_pool_size Tuning
children.description: The InnoDB buffer pool should be set to 70-80% of total RAM on a dedicated MySQL server, as it is by far the most impactful single memory setting — more buffer pool means more data and index pages cached in memory, reducing physical I/O that is orders of magnitude slower than RAM access. On servers with more than 1 GB of RAM, splitting the buffer pool into multiple instances with innodb_buffer_pool_instances improves concurrency by reducing mutex contention between threads accessing different areas of the pool. Monitoring Innodb_buffer_pool_read_requests versus Innodb_buffer_pool_reads gives you the cache hit ratio to validate whether your sizing is adequate for your working set.

children.title: Per-Thread Memory Settings
children.description: MySQL allocates per-thread buffers — sort_buffer_size, join_buffer_size, read_buffer_size — for each connection that performs the corresponding operation, meaning these settings are multiplied by max_connections in the worst case and can cause OOM kills if set too large. Setting these conservatively (256K–1M) for OLTP workloads and relying on good indexes to avoid sorting and full-table joins is the correct approach; only increase them for analytics workloads with justified need. Understanding the total memory budget — buffer pool + connection threads + OS cache — prevents you from overcommitting the server and causing paging.

children.title: innodb_log_file_size
children.description: The InnoDB redo log size controls how much write workload can be buffered before MySQL must checkpoint dirty pages to disk — a redo log that is too small relative to write throughput causes frequent checkpoint pressure and associated write stalls. In MySQL 8.0.30+, the redo log is dynamically resizable without restart, but in earlier versions changing it requires a controlled server restart with the old log files removed. A good starting point is 1-2 GB for write-heavy OLTP workloads, adjusted up if you observe high checkpoint activity in the InnoDB status output.

children.title: innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit
children.description: This single variable controls the durability-versus-performance trade-off for InnoDB: setting it to 1 (the default) flushes and syncs the redo log on every commit, guaranteeing no committed transaction loss but with maximum fsync overhead; setting it to 2 flushes but does not sync, risking up to one second of data loss on OS crash; and setting it to 0 risks up to one second of data loss even on MySQL crash. Most production OLTP systems should use 1 for full ACID compliance, while replica servers or analytics instances can safely use 2 for significantly better write throughput. Pairing this with sync_binlog=1 for binary log durability completes the write durability configuration.

children.title: max_connections and Thread Caching
children.description: Setting max_connections too high on a server with limited RAM can cause OOM kills when all connections simultaneously allocate their per-thread buffers, yet setting it too low causes connection refusals during traffic spikes that are just as harmful to availability. Thread caching via thread_cache_size amortizes the cost of thread creation and destruction for short-lived connections, reducing CPU overhead on high-connection-rate workloads. In practice, max_connections should be set to what the server can realistically handle with all threads active, and connection pooling at the application layer or via ProxySQL should absorb spikes above that.

children.title: sysbench Benchmarking
children.description: sysbench provides standardized OLTP benchmarks that simulate realistic read-write, read-only, and write-only workloads, letting you measure transactions-per-second and query latency percentiles before and after configuration changes with statistical confidence. Without benchmarking, configuration tuning is guesswork — a change that seems reasonable might actually regress throughput in your specific workload. Running sysbench at your expected production concurrency level with a dataset size that exceeds the buffer pool is the most realistic way to validate tuning changes before applying them to production.

children.title: File System and Mount Options
children.description: The file system and mount options under MySQL's data directory have significant impact on I/O durability and throughput — XFS is generally preferred over ext4 for large databases due to better handling of large files and concurrent writes, and mounting with noatime eliminates unnecessary writes for every file read. Disabling OS-level barriers when your storage has a battery-backed write cache safely improves write throughput, but enabling this on storage without a write cache risks data corruption on power failure. These OS-level tuning decisions complement MySQL configuration changes and should be part of any production server build standard.

children.title: innodb_io_capacity Tuning
children.description: innodb_io_capacity tells InnoDB how many I/O operations per second the storage system can handle, which the engine uses to throttle background tasks like flushing dirty pages and purging undo logs. Setting this too low on fast NVMe storage causes InnoDB to flush too conservatively, allowing dirty page accumulation that triggers burst I/O spikes; setting it too high on slow spinning disk overloads the disk with background I/O and starves user queries. Benchmarking raw disk I/O with fio and setting innodb_io_capacity to match actual measured throughput gives InnoDB an accurate model of its storage subsystem.

mysql-dba.title: Group Replication and InnoDB Cluster
mysql-dba.description: Configure MySQL Group Replication for multi-primary or single-primary fault-tolerant setups. Use MySQL Shell and MySQL Router to build a full InnoDB Cluster.
mysql-dba.challenge: Deploy a 3-node InnoDB Cluster using MySQL Shell, simulate a primary failure, and verify automatic failover with MySQL Router re-routing connections.

children.title: Group Replication Concepts
children.description: MySQL Group Replication uses the Paxos-based GCS (Group Communication System) protocol to achieve distributed consensus on transaction ordering before committing, eliminating the data loss risk of asynchronous replication. In single-primary mode, one node accepts writes and the others are read-only hot standbys ready to be promoted automatically on primary failure without manual intervention. Understanding the certification-based conflict detection mechanism — where each transaction's writeset is compared against concurrent transactions' readsets — explains why write-heavy multi-primary setups have higher abort rates than single-primary deployments.

children.title: Single-Primary vs Multi-Primary Mode
children.description: Single-primary mode is the recommended and safer Group Replication deployment because all writes go through one certified node, avoiding inter-node transaction certification conflicts entirely. Multi-primary mode allows writes on all nodes simultaneously but requires applications to handle transaction rollbacks caused by conflicting updates on different nodes, which is operationally complex and often not worth the added write throughput for OLTP workloads. Choosing the right mode at cluster creation time matters because converting between modes requires coordinating all cluster members.

children.title: MySQL Shell AdminAPI
children.description: MySQL Shell's AdminAPI abstracts away the complexity of configuring Group Replication directly, providing JavaScript and Python methods like dba.createCluster(), dba.addInstance(), and cluster.status() that handle the low-level configuration and health checking automatically. Using AdminAPI instead of manual my.cnf configuration ensures that all cluster members receive consistent and correct Group Replication parameters, reducing the risk of misconfiguration that causes split-brain or silent replication errors. AdminAPI commands also handle the bootstrapping sequence and GTID synchronization that must occur in a specific order when adding members to an existing cluster.

resources.label: InnoDB Cluster Setup with MySQL Shell

children.title: Handling Member Failures
children.description: InnoDB Cluster can tolerate floor((n-1)/2) member failures while maintaining a write quorum, meaning a 3-node cluster can sustain one failure and a 5-node cluster can sustain two. When a node loses connectivity, Group Replication uses the GCS view change protocol to expel the absent member after a configurable timeout, triggering automatic primary election if the primary was the failed node. Understanding how to safely re-join a recovered member — ensuring its GTID set is caught up before rejoining via dba.rebootClusterFromCompleteOutage() — prevents split-brain when a previously primary node restarts after a partition heals.

children.title: MySQL Router for Load Balancing
children.description: MySQL Router is a lightweight middleware that sits between applications and the InnoDB Cluster, routing read-write connections to the current primary and read-only connections to available secondaries without requiring application-level awareness of cluster topology changes. After a failover, MySQL Router automatically discovers the new primary through its metadata cache and redirects connections within seconds, providing transparent failover to applications that just reconnect. Router's configuration is bootstrapped automatically from a running cluster using --bootstrap, which generates the correct routing configuration based on the current cluster topology.

children.title: Read-Write Splitting with Router
children.description: MySQL Router's read-write splitting routes SELECT queries to secondary nodes and DML to the primary, distributing read load horizontally across the cluster without any application code changes. Applications connect to a single Router endpoint and Router handles the connection classification internally, making it transparent to ORMs and database abstraction layers that do not have native read-write splitting support. The read replica round-robin distribution strategy can be customized and combined with session-level routing hints for transactions that require reading freshly written data from the primary.

children.title: Cluster Status and Health Checks
children.description: The cluster.status() AdminAPI command provides a comprehensive JSON view of every cluster member's role, state, and replication health, making it the primary diagnostic tool for InnoDB Cluster operations. Members can be in ONLINE, RECOVERING, UNREACHABLE, or ERROR states, each requiring different operator responses from waiting for recovery to complete to forcefully removing and re-provisioning a member. Scripting regular cluster.status() health checks and alerting on any non-ONLINE member state transforms cluster monitoring from a manual task into an automated safety net.

children.title: Rolling Upgrades in InnoDB Cluster
children.description: InnoDB Cluster supports rolling minor version upgrades by removing one member at a time, upgrading it, and re-adding it to the cluster — maintaining availability throughout the process because the remaining members maintain quorum. Major version upgrades require more care because Group Replication does not support mixed major versions in the same cluster; a full cluster upgrade requires a maintenance window with a controlled switchover strategy. Understanding upgrade compatibility matrices and testing the upgrade sequence in a staging cluster before production minimizes the risk of version-mismatch errors that require emergency rollbacks.

mysql-dba.title: Production Operations and Disaster Recovery
mysql-dba.description: Operate MySQL at production scale using ProxySQL for connection pooling and query routing, and Orchestrator for topology management and automated failover. Implement a full DR plan.
mysql-dba.challenge: Set up ProxySQL in front of a replicated MySQL cluster, configure read-write splitting rules, trigger a failover via Orchestrator, and verify zero query routing errors.

children.title: ProxySQL Setup and Query Rules
children.description: ProxySQL is a high-performance MySQL proxy that sits in the data path and provides connection pooling, query routing, read-write splitting, query caching, and query rewriting — a Swiss Army knife for production MySQL operations. Connection pooling alone, which multiplexes thousands of application connections over a small number of backend connections, is often the single most impactful change a team can make to stabilize a MySQL server under connection spike conditions. Query rules configured in ProxySQL's runtime configuration allow you to route, mirror, or block specific query patterns without application changes, enabling gradual traffic shifting between database instances during migrations.

children.title: ProxySQL Query Routing Rules
children.description: ProxySQL's query routing rules match incoming queries against regex patterns and route them to specific hostgroups — read hostgroup for SELECTs, write hostgroup for DML — providing transparent read-write splitting without any application changes. The runtime_mysql_query_rules table is queryable and modifiable at runtime without restarting ProxySQL, enabling dynamic traffic management during incidents or gradual blue-green database migrations. Understanding the rule matching order, the apply flag, and the destination_hostgroup parameter lets you build sophisticated multi-tier routing hierarchies for complex database topologies.

children.title: Orchestrator for Failover
children.description: Orchestrator is a MySQL topology manager and automated failover tool that continuously maps the replication topology, detects primary failures, and promotes the most-up-to-date replica as the new primary without requiring manual intervention. Its Raft-based consensus mode prevents split-brain failovers by requiring a majority of Orchestrator nodes to agree that the primary is dead before initiating promotion, avoiding false failovers due to network partitions that only affect Orchestrator's visibility. Orchestrator's HTTP API and hooks allow integration with ProxySQL to update hostgroup membership after failover, creating a fully automated recovery pipeline.

children.title: pt-online-schema-change
children.description: ALTER TABLE on large tables in MySQL acquires a metadata lock that blocks all writes for the duration of the DDL operation — for tables with tens or hundreds of millions of rows, this can mean minutes or hours of write downtime. pt-online-schema-change from Percona Toolkit performs schema changes by creating a shadow table with the new schema, copying rows in configurable chunks, keeping the shadow synchronized via triggers, and performing a fast table swap at the end — all without blocking writes. Understanding its throttling options and chunk size configuration is necessary to prevent it from overloading the server during business hours.

children.title: DR Runbook Design
children.description: A disaster recovery runbook documents every step an on-call engineer must execute to recover a failed MySQL environment to a state where applications can resume — from detecting the incident through restoring from backup, validating data integrity, and handing off to the application team. Runbooks that are not tested regularly drift out of date as infrastructure changes, so scheduling quarterly DR drills that follow the runbook exactly under realistic time pressure is essential for maintaining actual recovery capability. The runbook should include explicit decision trees for different failure scenarios — primary-only failure versus datacenter loss versus data corruption — because the correct response differs significantly.

children.title: RPO and RTO Planning
children.description: Recovery Point Objective (RPO) defines the maximum acceptable data loss in time — one hour means you can lose up to one hour of transactions — while Recovery Time Objective (RTO) defines how long the database can be unavailable before the business impact is unacceptable. These objectives must be agreed upon with business stakeholders before they can be translated into technical requirements: an RPO of 1 hour suggests hourly backups or binary log shipping, while a 5-minute RPO requires near-real-time replication to a geographically separate replica. Without explicit, tested RPO and RTO commitments, a DBA team cannot know whether their backup and HA architecture is adequate until an actual disaster reveals the gap.

resources.label: RPO and RTO for Databases Explained

children.title: gh-ost for Online DDL
children.description: gh-ost (GitHub's Online Schema Transmogrifier) is an alternative to pt-online-schema-change that uses binary log streaming instead of triggers to capture ongoing changes during a schema migration, eliminating the write overhead and deadlock risk that triggers impose on the original table. Its built-in throttling based on replica lag, thread count, and custom status thresholds makes it safer to run on busy production servers without degrading application performance. gh-ost's interactive socket interface lets an operator dynamically pause, resume, or abort a running migration, providing operational control that is essential for long-running migrations on petabyte-scale tables.

children.title: SLA and Capacity Management
children.description: Defining explicit SLAs for MySQL availability, query latency, and replication lag gives the operations team clear targets and creates accountability for the reliability work that is otherwise invisible. Capacity planning based on historical growth trends — CPU, disk, IOPS, and connection counts — allows infrastructure provisioning to happen before limits are hit rather than in emergency response mode. Regularly reviewing SLA adherence metrics and capacity forecasts in a monthly operations review turns database reliability from reactive firefighting into a proactive engineering discipline.

mongodb-admin.title: MongoDB Fundamentals
mongodb-admin.description: Learn the document model of MongoDB and how it differs from relational databases. Install MongoDB, use mongosh, and perform basic CRUD operations on collections.
mongodb-admin.challenge: Create a MongoDB database for a blog platform with posts, comments, and users collections, and perform all CRUD operations using mongosh.

children.title: Document Model and BSON
children.description: MongoDB stores data as BSON (Binary JSON) documents, which allows you to represent rich hierarchical structures that map directly to objects in your application code. Understanding BSON types — including ObjectId, Date, Decimal128, and Binary — is essential because they control storage size, sorting behavior, and query accuracy. Misusing types (e.g., storing numbers as strings) leads to subtle bugs that are difficult to trace in production.

children.title: Installing MongoDB and mongosh
children.description: A correct local installation establishes the baseline for everything that follows — misconfigured data directories or wrong permissions will cause cryptic startup failures. mongosh (the modern MongoDB Shell) replaces the legacy mongo shell and provides JavaScript-based REPL access to your clusters with better autocompletion and scripting support. Getting comfortable with mongosh early makes day-to-day administration and ad-hoc queries much faster.

children.title: MongoDB Compass GUI
children.description: Compass provides a visual interface for exploring schema distribution, running queries with a query bar, and inspecting index usage without writing shell commands. It is especially valuable during the learning phase because it makes document structure and embedded arrays immediately visible. The built-in schema analyzer also surfaces field cardinality and type mismatches that would otherwise be hidden in raw documents.

children.title: Basic CRUD Operations
children.description: CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) is the foundation of every application that uses MongoDB, and a solid grasp of insertOne, findOne, updateOne, and deleteOne is required before moving to aggregations or admin tasks. Understanding the difference between updateOne with $set versus document replacement is critical because accidental replacements silently delete fields not included in the update. Practicing with real data sets builds the muscle memory needed for production troubleshooting.

children.title: MongoDB University M001
children.description: MongoDB University's M001 course is the official beginner course designed by MongoDB engineers and kept up to date with each major release. It combines interactive labs with a free Atlas cluster so you practice on real infrastructure rather than a simulated environment. Completing M001 earns a verified certificate that demonstrates competency to employers and is a prerequisite for the MongoDB Associate Developer certification.

children.title: Free Atlas Cluster Setup
children.description: MongoDB Atlas Free Tier (M0) provides a production-like environment on AWS, GCP, or Azure at no cost, making it the fastest way to get a cluster that mirrors real-world constraints. Running your experiments on Atlas rather than a local instance forces you to learn connection strings, network access lists, and database users — all of which are required skills for any production role. The Atlas UI also exposes monitoring dashboards that are invisible when running locally.

children.title: Sample Datasets
children.description: MongoDB Atlas ships with several sample datasets (mflix, airbnb, restaurants, weather) that represent realistic document shapes with embedded arrays and references. Working with these datasets gives you immediate context for why the document model differs from relational tables, because a movie document embedding its cast array is more natural than a join table. Using familiar domains also makes it easier to judge whether a query result is correct.

children.title: mongosh Scripting Basics
children.description: mongosh scripts let you automate repetitive tasks — seeding test data, running health checks, or exporting specific fields — without an external driver. Because mongosh is a full JavaScript environment, you can use loops, functions, and async/await, which makes it practical for writing small administrative scripts that are version-controlled alongside your application code. Learning scripting early means you can automate the manual steps you practice now.

mongodb-admin.title: Querying and Aggregation
mongodb-admin.description: Master MongoDB Query Language (MQL) for filtering documents and learn the aggregation pipeline for complex data transformations and analytics.
mongodb-admin.challenge: Write an aggregation pipeline that calculates total sales by category, filters categories with more than 100 sales, and sorts the result by revenue.

children.title: Comparison and Logical Operators
children.description: Operators like $gt, $in, $and, and $nor are the building blocks of every MongoDB query, and understanding their execution order determines whether your indexes will be hit or ignored. Choosing the wrong operator — such as $regex without an anchored prefix instead of a text index — can cause full collection scans that bring a production cluster to its knees. Mastering these operators lets you write precise queries that take the narrowest possible code path through your data.

children.title: Array and Embedded Document Queries
children.description: Querying arrays with $elemMatch and nested documents with dot notation are MongoDB-specific skills that have no direct SQL equivalent. Without $elemMatch, a query like { scores: { $gt: 80, $lt: 90 } } can match documents where different array elements satisfy each condition separately — a bug that only appears with multi-element arrays. Getting this right is critical for any schema that models one-to-many relationships through embedding.

children.title: $match, $group, and $project
children.description: Placing $match as early as possible in a pipeline is the single most impactful performance decision in aggregation because it reduces the number of documents that subsequent stages must process. $group with accumulators like $sum and $avg replaces the GROUP BY + aggregate function pattern from SQL, but without the automatic deduplication that SQL provides. $project controls the shape of output documents and can add computed fields using expressions, making it central to transforming raw data into report-ready structures.

children.title: $lookup and $unwind
children.description: $lookup is MongoDB's equivalent of a LEFT OUTER JOIN, enabling you to combine data from two collections in a single pipeline without application-side merging. $unwind deconstructs an array field into separate documents per element, which is a prerequisite for grouping or filtering on individual array elements within an aggregation. Understanding that $lookup on an unindexed field performs a collection scan on the joined collection is critical for avoiding slow pipelines in production.

children.title: $facet for Multi-Dimensional Analytics
children.description: $facet runs multiple sub-pipelines on the same input documents simultaneously, which is ideal for building faceted search results (e.g., count by category, price range, and rating) in a single database round-trip. Without $facet, you would need multiple separate queries and client-side merging, multiplying network overhead and potentially returning inconsistent snapshots if data changes between requests. It is the key operator for building analytics dashboards that need several aggregated views of the same dataset.

children.title: Aggregation Performance and explain()
children.description: Calling db.collection.explain() on an aggregation pipeline exposes the execution plan for each stage, revealing whether the $match stage uses an index or triggers a COLLSCAN. A pipeline that looks logically correct can still be catastrophically slow if $group precedes $match, forcing MongoDB to hash every document in the collection. Understanding how to read pipeline execution statistics — especially nReturned, totalDocsExamined, and executionTimeMillis — is essential for production query tuning.

children.title: MongoDB University M121
children.description: The MongoDB Aggregation Framework course (M121) teaches the pipeline from foundational stages up to advanced operators like $graphLookup and $bucket, with graded labs on real datasets. Completing it removes the guesswork from pipeline construction by teaching you to think in terms of document shape transformations rather than SQL set operations. The course also covers common pitfalls like memory limits on $sort and $group that would otherwise cause surprise failures on large collections.

children.title: Aggregation Builder in Compass
children.description: MongoDB Compass includes a visual Aggregation Pipeline Builder that previews the output of each stage as you add it, making it much easier to debug a pipeline than writing it blind in the shell. The builder can export the finished pipeline to mongosh, Python, Node.js, or Java driver syntax, which removes translation errors when moving from prototype to production code. It is particularly useful when learning $unwind followed by $group, because the intermediate document shape is immediately visible.

mongodb-admin.title: Indexing in MongoDB
mongodb-admin.description: Create and manage indexes in MongoDB to improve query performance. Use explain() to analyze query plans and understand when indexes are and are not used.
mongodb-admin.challenge: Identify a collection scan in a slow query using explain(), add appropriate indexes, and demonstrate a covered query that returns data from the index alone.

children.title: Single Field and Compound Indexes
children.description: A compound index on { status: 1, createdAt: -1 } can satisfy queries that filter on status alone, or on both status and createdAt, but not on createdAt alone — this is the index prefix rule that determines which queries an index covers. Getting the field order and sort direction wrong means a query that looks indexed will silently fall back to a collection scan, a regression that only shows up under load. Compound indexes also eliminate the need for sorting in memory when the index order matches the sort order in a query.

children.title: TTL and Multikey Indexes
children.description: TTL indexes automatically delete documents after a specified number of seconds, making them the correct tool for session data, audit logs, or notification queues with a finite retention period — replacing cron jobs that would otherwise require application-level expiry logic. Multikey indexes allow MongoDB to index every element of an array field, enabling efficient queries like 'find all documents where the tags array contains "mongodb"'. Understanding that a compound index cannot have two multikey fields is a constraint that must be considered at schema design time.

children.title: explain() and Winning Plan
children.description: Running db.collection.explain('executionStats') reveals whether a query used IXSCAN (index scan) or COLLSCAN (collection scan), how many documents were examined versus returned, and how long execution took. The ratio of totalDocsExamined to nReturned is a direct measure of index efficiency — a ratio of 1:1 means the index is perfectly selective, while a ratio of 10000:1 indicates a poorly targeted index or a missing filter condition. Fixing queries based on explain() output is the fastest path to eliminating slow-query alerts.

resources.label: How to Use explain() in MongoDB

children.title: Covered Queries and Index Hints
children.description: A covered query retrieves all requested fields directly from the index without touching the underlying collection documents, making it the fastest possible read path. Achieving coverage requires that all fields in the query filter, sort, and projection are included in a single index in the correct order. Index hints with db.collection.find({}).hint({ field: 1 }) override the query planner's automatic selection, which is useful when the planner chooses a suboptimal plan under certain data distributions.

children.title: Text Indexes for Full-Text Search
children.description: A text index tokenizes string fields and stores stemmed terms, enabling queries like db.articles.find({ $text: { $search: "database performance" } }) without a regular expression. Text indexes support weighted fields so a match in the title field can rank higher than a match in the body, producing relevance-ordered results. Understanding that only one text index is allowed per collection is important at schema design time, because all text-searchable fields must be combined into a single index.

resources.label: Full-Text Search with MongoDB Text Index

children.title: Geospatial Indexes (2dsphere)
children.description: 2dsphere indexes store GeoJSON geometries and support queries like $near (find points within a radius), $geoWithin (find points inside a polygon), and $geoIntersects (find geometries that overlap). Location-based features — store finders, delivery zones, ride-sharing — require geospatial indexes because coordinate arithmetic on non-indexed fields would require a full collection scan on every request. Understanding the coordinate order convention (longitude, latitude in GeoJSON) is critical because swapping them produces silently wrong distance calculations.

children.title: MongoDB University M201
children.description: The MongoDB Performance course (M201) covers index internals, execution plan selection, and advanced optimization techniques including index intersection and query shape analysis. It provides the conceptual framework for understanding why the query planner makes the choices it does, which is necessary for diagnosing cases where the planner picks a worse plan under high concurrency. This course is the most direct path to answering "why is this indexed query still slow?" questions.

children.title: Rolling Index Builds
children.description: Building indexes in a replica set can create replication lag and increased load on the primary if not managed carefully. A rolling index build — creating the index on each secondary in sequence while it is in standalone mode, then on the primary — eliminates the write-pause penalty of a foreground index build on large collections. Knowing this procedure is mandatory before touching indexes on any production replica set where downtime is not acceptable.

mongodb-admin.title: Schema Design and Data Modeling
mongodb-admin.description: Design MongoDB schemas by choosing between embedding and referencing documents based on access patterns. Apply design patterns like bucket, polymorphic, and outlier.
mongodb-admin.challenge: Design a schema for an IoT sensor platform storing time-series readings, apply the bucket pattern to reduce document count, and compare query performance.

children.title: Choosing Between Embedding and References
children.description: The fundamental schema design decision in MongoDB is whether to embed related data inside a document or store it in a separate collection linked by an ObjectId reference. Embedding wins when data is always accessed together, has bounded size, and is owned by a single parent — embedding eliminates the extra $lookup query. Referencing wins when the related data is large, shared across many parents, or independently queried, because embedding would duplicate data and make updates inconsistent across all copies.

children.title: One-to-Many Relationships
children.description: In a one-to-many relationship, the cardinality of the "many" side determines whether embedding is viable — embedding 5 order line items per order is reasonable, but embedding millions of sensor readings per device document is not because MongoDB documents are capped at 16 MB. The extended reference pattern (embedding a subset of the related document's fields rather than the full document) balances the need for a single read with keeping document size manageable. Choosing the right cardinality boundary is the most important schema design decision because changing it in production requires a full collection migration.

children.title: Bucket Pattern for Time-Series
children.description: The bucket pattern groups a sequence of measurements — for example, one hour of IoT sensor readings — into a single document with an array of sub-documents, dramatically reducing the total document count and improving compression. Without bucketing, an IoT application writing 1,000 readings per second creates 86.4 million documents per day, each with its own document metadata overhead. Bucketing reduces that to 24,000 documents per day (one per bucket per hour) while enabling fast range aggregations because fewer documents need to be opened.

children.title: Polymorphic and Schema Versioning Patterns
children.description: The polymorphic pattern stores documents of different shapes in one collection, identified by a type discriminator field — useful for product catalogs where books, electronics, and clothing share some fields but each have unique attributes. The schema versioning pattern adds a schemaVersion field to every document, enabling the application to handle multiple document shapes gracefully during a gradual migration rather than requiring a big-bang schema change. Both patterns are critical for operating MongoDB in a system where the schema evolves over time without downtime.

children.title: Identifying Access Patterns First
children.description: Unlike relational databases where you normalize first and optimize later, MongoDB schema design starts with a complete list of how the application will query and update data. Without documenting access patterns upfront, you will inevitably design a schema that forces expensive $lookup joins or in-application merging for the most frequent operations. A simple table listing each access pattern, its frequency, and its latency requirement is the most valuable artifact produced during MongoDB schema design.

children.title: Outlier Pattern
children.description: The outlier pattern handles the case where a small number of documents have arrays that grow far beyond the typical size — for example, a celebrity user with 50 million followers versus an average user with 200. Without the outlier pattern, you are forced to use references for all users to accommodate the extreme case, which harms performance for the 99.9% of normal users. The pattern solves this by embedding up to a threshold (e.g., 1,000 followers) and adding an overflow flag that the application checks before fetching additional data from a separate collection.

children.title: MongoDB: The Definitive Guide
children.description: Chapters 8 and 9 of MongoDB: The Definitive Guide dedicate extensive coverage to schema design decisions, explaining how the document model's flexibility is both its greatest strength and its greatest risk if not disciplined by access patterns. The book bridges theory and practice by showing real-world examples of schemas that evolved incorrectly and the migration paths used to fix them. Reading these chapters before building a production schema can prevent months of painful data migrations.

children.title: MongoDB Compass Schema Analyzer
children.description: Compass's Schema tab samples documents in a collection and visualizes the field distribution, type mix, and cardinality for every field — revealing where the schema has drifted from its intended design. Discovering that a field intended to be a Date has accumulated String values in 5% of documents, or that an optional field is missing in 40% of documents, helps prioritize which schema inconsistencies to address before they become query correctness bugs. Running the schema analyzer regularly on production collections is a lightweight form of ongoing schema governance.

mongodb-admin.title: Backup and Security
mongodb-admin.description: Implement backup and restore procedures using mongodump and mongorestore or Atlas Backup. Secure MongoDB with role-based access control and TLS encryption.
mongodb-admin.challenge: Enable RBAC with least-privilege roles, configure TLS for client connections, perform a full mongodump backup, drop a collection, and restore it.

children.title: mongodump and mongorestore
children.description: mongodump exports a logical backup of your data as BSON files that can be selectively restored by database, collection, or even query filter — making it the right tool for migrations, data archaeology, and collection-level restores. Because mongodump reads live data through the query engine, it is consistent at the document level but not cluster-wide, so restores may reflect documents written at slightly different moments during the dump. Running mongodump against a secondary rather than the primary avoids adding backup I/O pressure to the node handling application writes.

children.title: Atlas Backup and Continuous Cloud Backup
children.description: Atlas Continuous Cloud Backup captures snapshots of your cluster's oplog every few minutes, enabling point-in-time recovery to any second within the configured retention window — not just to snapshot boundaries. This is critical for recovering from accidental bulk deletes or application bugs that corrupt data over a period of minutes, where you want to restore to the last known good state rather than the last nightly snapshot. Atlas manages backup storage, encryption at rest, and restore orchestration, removing the operational burden of running your own backup infrastructure.

children.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
children.description: MongoDB's RBAC system grants privileges through roles, and applying least-privilege means creating a role for each distinct application persona rather than sharing credentials. A read-only analytics role, a write role for the application service, and a backup role for the backup agent should all be separate users with separate credentials, so a compromise of one does not expose all operations. Built-in roles like readAnyDatabase are conveniences for development but should never be used in production because they violate the principle of least privilege.

children.title: Custom Roles and Privilege Management
children.description: When built-in roles are too broad, custom roles let you combine individual actions (find, insert, update, createIndex) on specific resources (a named collection or database) into a precisely scoped permission set. This granularity is required in regulated industries where auditors need proof that no application user has permission to drop collections or read data outside their service boundary. Version-controlling role definitions in a script that is re-applied as part of deployment ensures that privilege creep is caught in code review.

children.title: TLS/SSL for Client Connections
children.description: Enabling TLS encrypts all data in transit between clients, application servers, and replica set members, preventing credential sniffing and man-in-the-middle attacks on your database traffic. MongoDB requires TLS certificates signed by a CA that all parties trust, and using self-signed certificates in production is acceptable only if the CA cert is distributed to all clients as part of a controlled deployment process. Without TLS, any network-level attacker between your application and MongoDB can read query results, including sensitive personal or financial data.

children.title: Client-Side Field Level Encryption
children.description: Client-Side Field Level Encryption (CSFLE) encrypts individual document fields in the driver before data leaves the application, so even the database server never sees plaintext for sensitive fields like SSN, credit card numbers, or health data. This means that even if an attacker gains direct access to the MongoDB server or its storage volumes, the encrypted fields are unreadable without the encryption keys held by the application. CSFLE is the correct approach for meeting compliance requirements (HIPAA, PCI-DSS) where encryption at rest alone is not sufficient because it protects data from insiders with database access.

children.title: Security Checklist and Auditing
children.description: MongoDB's official security checklist covers bind IP restriction (--bind_ip should never be 0.0.0.0 in production), enabling authentication, and disabling the HTTP interface — all of which were exposed in the 2017 "MongoDB apocalypse" that exposed tens of thousands of databases to ransomware. The MongoDB Auditing feature (available in Enterprise and Atlas) logs authentication, authorization, and DDL operations to a tamper-evident log, which is required for SOC 2 and ISO 27001 compliance audits. Running through the checklist before go-live is a non-negotiable step in production preparation.

children.title: Encryption at Rest with WiredTiger
children.description: MongoDB Enterprise's encrypted storage engine encrypts data files on disk using AES-256, protecting backup tapes, stolen disks, and cloud storage snapshots from unauthorized data access. The encryption key is managed separately from the data — typically via a Key Management Interoperability Protocol (KMIP) server — so rotating the key is possible without decrypting and re-encrypting all data. Atlas enables encryption at rest by default with AWS KMS, GCP KMS, or Azure Key Vault integration, which fulfills the encryption at rest requirement of most compliance frameworks without additional configuration.

mongodb-admin.title: Replica Sets
mongodb-admin.description: Deploy a MongoDB replica set for high availability and data redundancy. Understand the oplog, election process, and read preference options for distributing load.
mongodb-admin.challenge: Deploy a 3-member replica set, step down the primary, observe the election, and configure an application to route reads to secondary nodes.

children.title: Primary Election and Oplog
children.description: When the primary becomes unavailable, the remaining voting members hold an election using the Raft consensus algorithm, and the secondary with the most up-to-date oplog position typically wins. The oplog (operations log) is a capped collection in the local database that records every write operation in an idempotent format so secondaries can replay it to stay synchronized. Sizing the oplog too small means a secondary that falls behind (e.g., during a maintenance window) can no longer catch up without a full resync, causing unexpected downtime.

children.title: Write Concerns
children.description: Write concern { w: 'majority' } ensures that a write is acknowledged only after it has been replicated to a majority of voting members, making it durable even if the primary crashes immediately after the acknowledgment. The default write concern of { w: 1 } only confirms the write reached the primary's in-memory journal, meaning data written but not yet replicated can be lost in a failover. For financial systems or any data where loss is unacceptable, majority write concern is mandatory despite its slightly higher latency.

children.title: Read Preferences
children.description: Read preference controls which replica set member receives a read operation: primaryPreferred routes to the primary unless it is unavailable, secondary routes to secondaries to offload the primary, and nearest routes to the member with the lowest network latency regardless of role. Using secondary read preference for analytics queries distributes load and prevents reporting workloads from competing with write operations on the primary. The tradeoff is that secondary reads may return stale data if replication lag is high, so applications must tolerate eventual consistency when using non-primary read preferences.

children.title: Replica Set Maintenance Operations
children.description: Rolling restarts — stepping down the primary, restarting each secondary, and then restarting the new primary — allow MongoDB version upgrades and configuration changes with zero downtime. Using rs.stepDown() before restarting the primary is critical because it triggers a controlled election, giving secondaries time to catch up on the oplog before the primary disappears. Skipping the step-down causes clients to experience a write freeze that lasts until the election completes, which is typically 10-30 seconds.

resources.label: Perform Maintenance on Replica Set Members

children.title: Deploying a 3-Member Replica Set
children.description: A 3-member replica set (one primary and two secondaries) is the minimum recommended configuration because it provides a majority of two votes required for an election even when one member is down. Running only two members requires an arbiter to reach a voting majority, but arbiters do not hold data copies, so the cluster has no redundancy — a single secondary failure leaves the system with one data-bearing node. For production deployments, placing each member in a different availability zone or rack provides protection against infrastructure-level failures.

resources.label: Setting Up a MongoDB Replica Set

children.title: Oplog Monitoring and Sizing
children.description: Monitoring the replication lag (the difference between the primary's oplog timestamp and each secondary's applied timestamp) is critical because a secondary that falls too far behind cannot serve reads reliably and may not recover before the oplog window closes. The rs.printReplicationInfo() command shows the oplog's current size and the time range it covers, which is the maximum time a secondary can be offline before requiring a resync. For write-heavy workloads, increasing the oplog size with --oplogSize is a proactive measure that prevents replication failures during periods of high write throughput.

resources.label: Check the Size of the Oplog

children.title: Hidden and Delayed Members
children.description: A hidden replica set member participates in elections (by setting votes to 0, it is excluded) but is invisible to clients, making it ideal for running analytics or ETL jobs without competing with application reads. A delayed member replicates the oplog with a configurable lag — for example, 1 hour behind — providing a built-in point-in-time recovery window for accidental deletions that mongorestore would not catch until the next backup cycle. Both member types add resilience to the cluster without requiring additional backup infrastructure.

children.title: MongoDB: The Definitive Guide — Replication Chapter
children.description: Chapter 10 of MongoDB: The Definitive Guide covers replica set deployment, election algorithms, and operational scenarios including how to recover from common failure modes like network partitions and corrupted data files. The book explains the oplog mechanics in enough depth to reason about edge cases — such as what happens when a secondary's oplog pointer falls off the end of the primary's oplog — that are not covered in the official documentation. This level of understanding is required for diagnosing incidents that go beyond the standard runbook.

mongodb-admin.title: Sharding and Horizontal Scaling
mongodb-admin.description: Scale MongoDB horizontally by configuring a sharded cluster with config servers and mongos routers. Choose shard keys strategically to avoid hotspots.
mongodb-admin.challenge: Deploy a 2-shard cluster with config servers and mongos, enable sharding on a collection with a hashed shard key, and verify even chunk distribution.

children.title: Choosing an Effective Shard Key
children.description: The shard key determines how data is distributed across shards, and a poor choice — such as a monotonically increasing field like a timestamp — creates a hotspot where all new writes go to a single shard while others sit idle. A good shard key has high cardinality (many distinct values), low correlation with insert order (to avoid hotspots), and aligns with the most frequent query filters (to enable targeted queries rather than broadcast scatter-gather). Changing a shard key requires resharding the entire collection, which is a major operational event, so getting it right the first time is essential.

resources.label: Choose a Shard Key - MongoDB Manual

children.title: Hashed vs Ranged Sharding
children.description: Hashed sharding computes a hash of the shard key value and distributes chunks evenly across shards based on the hash, which eliminates hotspots even for monotonically increasing keys like ObjectId at the cost of making range queries broadcast to all shards. Ranged sharding preserves the natural order of the shard key, enabling efficient range queries that target a single shard, but requires careful shard key selection to avoid hot chunk accumulation. Choosing between them depends on whether range queries or write distribution is more critical for your specific workload.

children.title: mongos Router and Config Servers
children.description: The mongos router is a stateless process that receives client queries, consults the config servers to determine which shards hold the relevant chunks, and routes operations to the correct shard(s) — clients connect to mongos, never directly to individual shards. Config servers store the cluster metadata — the shard key space, chunk boundaries, and shard membership — in a replica set, and losing them permanently makes the cluster's chunk distribution unrecoverable. Running multiple mongos instances behind a load balancer ensures that the routing layer itself is not a single point of failure.

children.title: Chunk Balancing
children.description: The MongoDB balancer automatically migrates chunks between shards to keep data distribution even, but chunk migrations consume network and I/O bandwidth that can degrade query performance if the balancer runs during peak hours. Configuring a balancer window to restrict migrations to off-peak hours prevents the balancer from interfering with production traffic while still maintaining a balanced cluster over time. Monitoring the number of chunks per shard with sh.status() helps you detect shard key hotspots before they cause noticeable performance degradation.

children.title: Sharding a Live Collection
children.description: Sharding an existing collection requires the shard key field to have an index already in place, and MongoDB will not let you shard a collection without it — the initial indexing on a large collection must be planned and scheduled carefully. After enableSharding() and shardCollection(), the balancer begins migrating chunks which can take hours on large collections and temporarily increases read latency as chunks are split and moved. Having a maintenance window or throttling strategy for the initial balancing phase is necessary to avoid impacting production query performance.

children.title: Tag-Aware Sharding (Zone Sharding)
children.description: Tag-aware sharding assigns specific chunk ranges to specific shards using zone tags, enabling data locality requirements such as keeping European user data on EU-region shards for GDPR compliance. It is also used for tiered storage — routing hot recent data to high-performance SSD shards and cold archival data to cheaper HDD shards based on date ranges. Without zone sharding, the balancer distributes data randomly across all shards, making it impossible to enforce data residency or storage tier requirements.

resources.label: MongoDB Zone Sharding for Data Locality

children.title: sh.status() and Cluster Diagnostics
children.description: sh.status() provides a snapshot of the sharded cluster's topology — each shard's replica set members, the chunk count per shard, and the shard key ranges — making it the first command to run when investigating imbalanced load or data distribution issues. The balancerState field shows whether the balancer is active, and the number of chunks per collection is a key indicator of whether a collection needs a presplit strategy to avoid the initial single-chunk period after sharding. Running sh.status() before and after operational changes is a good habit that catches misconfigurations early.

children.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
children.description: Chapter 6 of Designing Data-Intensive Applications by Martin Kleppmann provides the most rigorous conceptual treatment of partitioning (sharding) strategies available outside of academic papers, covering secondary index challenges in partitioned systems that MongoDB's scatter-gather behavior exemplifies. Understanding the theoretical trade-offs between global secondary indexes and local secondary indexes explains why certain MongoDB query patterns against a sharded collection are expensive and how to restructure the schema to avoid them. This background makes you a more effective architect for any distributed data system, not just MongoDB.

mongodb-admin.title: Performance Monitoring and Tuning
mongodb-admin.description: Monitor MongoDB performance using built-in tools and Atlas dashboards. Identify slow operations, analyze the profiler output, and tune configuration parameters.
mongodb-admin.challenge: Enable the database profiler at level 1, load test the cluster, identify the top 3 slow operations, and optimize them with indexes or query rewrites.

children.title: mongostat and mongotop
children.description: mongostat samples the mongod process every second and prints metrics including inserts/sec, queries/sec, updates/sec, memory usage, and connection count — giving an immediate live picture of what a server is doing without any setup. mongotop breaks down time spent per collection in read and write operations, pinpointing exactly which collection is causing the most I/O during a performance incident. Running both tools during a load test is the fastest way to validate whether tuning changes produced a measurable throughput improvement.

resources.label: MongoDB Monitoring with mongostat and mongotop

children.title: Database Profiler
children.description: MongoDB's database profiler at level 1 captures queries slower than the slowMs threshold (100ms by default) to the system.profile collection, enabling post-hoc analysis of slow operations without any application code changes. The profiler output includes the query shape, index used, documents examined, time in milliseconds, and the originating client, which is everything needed to root-cause a slow query. Setting the profiler to level 2 (log all operations) during a brief sampling window on a secondary is a safe way to get a complete workload fingerprint without impacting production writes.

children.title: WiredTiger Cache Configuration
children.description: The WiredTiger cache (configured via storage.wiredTiger.engineConfig.cacheSizeGB) holds frequently accessed data pages in memory, and sizing it too small forces constant disk reads that cause high read latency under any meaningful workload. MongoDB's default is 50% of RAM minus 1 GB, which is a reasonable starting point but should be tuned based on the ratio of working set size to available RAM revealed by Atlas metrics or the serverStatus command. Setting the cache too large on a host running other processes (application server, agent) causes memory pressure and thrashing.

children.title: currentOp and killOp
children.description: db.currentOp() returns all in-progress operations with their duration, query shape, and connection origin, making it the primary diagnostic tool when a server is under sudden unexpected load or when a runaway operation is blocking others. Long-running operations can be terminated with db.killOp(opId) when they are consuming excessive resources, but killing oplog replay operations on a secondary or an internal transaction can leave the server in an inconsistent state — only user-initiated operations should be killed without careful verification. Setting a maxTimeMS limit on queries is a better proactive approach that prevents any single operation from monopolizing the server.

children.title: Atlas Performance Advisor
children.description: Atlas Performance Advisor automatically analyzes queries executed against your cluster and recommends indexes based on query patterns, estimated performance improvement, and the queries' frequency and impact. The advisor scores each recommendation by the cumulative time that queries of that shape spent executing over the past 24 hours, so you can prioritize the indexes that will have the most immediate production impact. Accepting an advisor recommendation triggers an index build that runs as a background operation, minimizing disruption to ongoing queries.

children.title: Atlas Real-Time Performance Panel
children.description: The Atlas Real-Time Performance Panel streams live metrics including operations per second, network throughput, active connections, and query targeting ratio (the percentage of queries that use an index versus scatter-gather) updated every second. The query targeting ratio is one of the most actionable metrics on the dashboard because a ratio below 1.0 immediately signals that some queries are performing collection scans even though indexes exist. Atlas also surfaces hot collections and slow operations in the panel, eliminating the need to run mongostat and the profiler manually during a performance incident.

children.title: Prometheus mongodb_exporter
children.description: The community mongodb_exporter scrapes metrics from the MongoDB serverStatus command and exposes them in Prometheus format, enabling long-term storage and alerting through Alertmanager. Prometheus histograms for query latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) are more reliable than averages for detecting performance regressions because a single runaway query does not distort the p50 the way it distorts the mean. Having historical Prometheus data is also critical for capacity planning, providing the data to project when disk, connections, or cache will reach saturation.

children.title: MongoDB University M201 — Performance
children.description: M201 goes beyond query optimization into execution plan internals, covering how MongoDB's query planner caches plans, when the cache is invalidated, and how to force re-planning after schema changes. It also covers connection pool tuning, which is critical for applications that see connection spikes under bursty load — misconfigured connection pools are a common cause of performance degradation that looks like a database problem but is actually a driver configuration issue. The course provides a vocabulary for discussing performance issues precisely with other engineers.

mongodb-admin.title: Transactions and Change Streams
mongodb-admin.description: Use multi-document ACID transactions for operations that span multiple collections. Leverage change streams to build real-time event-driven applications.
mongodb-admin.challenge: Implement a fund transfer between two account documents using a multi-document transaction and build a change stream listener that logs all balance changes.

children.title: ACID Transaction Syntax and Sessions
children.description: MongoDB multi-document transactions group multiple read and write operations across collections into a single atomic unit that either fully commits or fully rolls back, providing the same guarantees that relational databases have always offered. Transactions require an explicit session object created with client.startSession(), and all operations within the transaction must reference that session — omitting the session on even one operation silently executes it outside the transaction without atomicity. Setting appropriate transaction timeouts (transactionLifetimeLimitSeconds) prevents transactions that are never committed from holding locks indefinitely.

children.title: Transaction Performance Considerations
children.description: Transactions in MongoDB hold intent locks on the documents they touch, and long-running transactions increase the probability of write conflicts that cause TransientTransactionError exceptions requiring client-side retry logic. The official MongoDB drivers provide a withTransaction() helper that automatically handles TransientTransactionError and UnknownTransactionCommitResult retries, and using it instead of manual try/catch/abort is strongly recommended. Keeping transactions short — under 1 second — and minimizing the number of documents touched per transaction are the two most important performance guidelines for maintaining throughput under load.

children.title: Change Stream Basics and Resume Tokens
children.description: Change streams provide a real-time ordered stream of data change events (insert, update, replace, delete, invalidate) from a collection, database, or entire deployment — built on the replica set oplog and therefore only available on replica sets and sharded clusters. Resume tokens allow a change stream listener that disconnects to resume from exactly where it stopped, ensuring no change events are missed during network interruptions or application restarts. Storing the resume token durably (in a database or file) before processing each event is critical because losing it means the stream cannot resume and requires reprocessing from the beginning.

children.title: Filtering Change Streams with Aggregation
children.description: Change streams accept an aggregation pipeline that filters and transforms events before they are delivered to the listener, which is far more efficient than receiving all events and filtering in the application layer. A pipeline like [{ $match: { operationType: 'insert', 'fullDocument.status': 'pending' } }] ensures the listener only wakes up for relevant events, reducing CPU usage and network traffic on both the server and the application. Using $project to include only the fields the listener needs also reduces payload size, which is important when change streams are used to trigger low-latency workflows.

resources.label: Modify Change Stream Output with Pipelines

children.title: Change Streams for CDC and Event Sourcing
children.description: Change streams are MongoDB's native Change Data Capture (CDC) mechanism, enabling downstream systems — search indexes, caches, data warehouses — to stay synchronized with the source of truth database without polling or dual-write logic. Using change streams to feed an event bus (Kafka, AWS EventBridge) decouples producers from consumers, allowing new consumers to be added without modifying the application. Understanding change stream guarantees — each event is delivered at least once, in oplog order — is necessary for designing idempotent consumers that handle duplicate delivery correctly.

resources.label: Building Event-Driven Apps with Change Streams

children.title: Kafka Connector for MongoDB
children.description: The official MongoDB Kafka Connector bridges change streams to Kafka topics with configurable output formats (BSON, JSON, Avro), providing the infrastructure layer for event-driven microservices that need reliable delivery guarantees. The sink connector allows Kafka topics to be written back to MongoDB collections, enabling bidirectional synchronization pipelines between MongoDB and other systems. Using the connector instead of a custom change stream consumer offloads offset management and partition assignment to Kafka, which eliminates most of the reliability engineering otherwise needed for a custom CDC pipeline.

children.title: Production Considerations for Transactions
children.description: MongoDB recommends using the majority read concern and majority write concern inside transactions to ensure that the committed data is durable even if the primary fails immediately after commit. Using a read concern of snapshot inside transactions provides the strongest consistency guarantee — the transaction sees a consistent snapshot of the data as it existed at the start of the transaction, preventing non-repeatable reads. The sharded cluster transaction limitation that all documents in a transaction must reside on the same shard (unless using a coordinator) is a key constraint that must be considered when designing cross-collection operations on a sharded system.

children.title: Transactions in Sharded Clusters
children.description: Sharded cluster transactions involve a two-phase commit coordinated by the mongos router, which adds latency compared to replica set transactions because the coordinator must communicate with each shard before committing. Ensuring that the shard key is chosen so that most transactions touch documents on a single shard dramatically reduces this overhead and avoids the performance cliff of cross-shard transactions. Understanding the $clusterTime mechanism that keeps all participants synchronized is necessary for diagnosing timeout failures in multi-shard transactions.

mongodb-admin.title: Atlas Operations and Production Readiness
mongodb-admin.description: Operate MongoDB at production scale using Atlas managed service, including cluster tier selection, automated backups, Atlas Search, and global distributed clusters.
mongodb-admin.challenge: Deploy a production-like Atlas cluster with a 3-region global setup, configure Atlas Search on a text field, set up backup policies, and simulate a region failure.

children.title: Cluster Tier Selection and Auto-Scaling
children.description: Atlas cluster tiers (M10 through M700+) define the CPU, RAM, and storage IOPS available to your cluster, and selecting the wrong tier — either too small causing latency spikes under load or too large causing unnecessary cost — is a common operational mistake. Auto-scaling allows Atlas to increase or decrease the cluster tier in response to CPU and memory utilization, eliminating the need for manual tier changes during traffic spikes. Understanding the trade-off between on-demand scaling agility and the cost predictability of fixed-tier provisioning is central to production cost management.

children.title: Atlas Alerts and Monitoring
children.description: Atlas has over 100 built-in alert conditions covering CPU utilization, replication lag, connection saturation, disk IOPS, query targeting ratio, and more — configuring the right subset of these alerts is what separates a proactively managed cluster from one that only gets attention after users report problems. Sending Atlas alerts to PagerDuty, Slack, or Opsgenie through webhook integrations ensures the right person is notified immediately without manual dashboard monitoring. Setting alert thresholds based on historical baseline metrics rather than arbitrary values reduces false positive fatigue that causes engineers to ignore alerts.

children.title: Atlas Search Index Creation
children.description: Atlas Search embeds an Apache Lucene search engine into your cluster, enabling full-text search, fuzzy matching, autocomplete, and faceted search without a separate Elasticsearch deployment. Defining an Atlas Search index specifies which fields to index, the analyzer to use for tokenization, and field-level weights for relevance scoring, all through a JSON mapping document. Because the search index is separate from the MongoDB query index, updating a document triggers an asynchronous sync to Lucene, meaning very recent writes may not appear in search results immediately — an eventual consistency tradeoff the application must account for.

children.title: Atlas Data Federation
children.description: Atlas Data Federation allows you to query data across Atlas clusters, AWS S3 buckets, Azure Blob Storage, and HTTP endpoints using MQL — a single interface that eliminates the need for separate ETL pipelines to consolidate data for reporting. Archiving old data from Atlas to S3 through the Online Archive feature reduces storage costs while keeping the data queryable through the same connection string, making tiered storage transparent to the application.
