children.description: In Cassandra, the partition key determines which node stores the data and must be chosen so that data is spread evenly and each partition is bounded in size—a partition growing without bound becomes a performance problem at compaction time. Clustering columns define the sort order within a partition and must be chosen to support the exact ORDER BY and range scan patterns the application requires, because sorting after the fact in application code defeats Cassandra's storage model. A partition key that is too selective produces millions of tiny partitions, while one that is too coarse creates hot partitions that funnel all traffic to one node.

children.title: Query-First Data Modeling
children.description: Unlike relational databases where you normalize data and let the query planner figure out joins, Cassandra requires you to know every query upfront and design a table for each one—there are no joins and no ad-hoc cross-partition queries. This query-first approach means a single logical entity may need to be stored in multiple denormalized tables to support lookups by different keys. Teams coming from SQL often resist this duplication until they encounter ALLOW FILTERING queries that scan every node in the cluster and silently degrade at scale.

children.title: Keyspace Configuration and Replication Strategies
children.description: A Cassandra keyspace is the top-level namespace containing tables and defines the replication strategy—SimpleStrategy for single-datacenter deployments and NetworkTopologyStrategy for multi-datacenter setups. NetworkTopologyStrategy must be used in production because it is aware of datacenter and rack topology, ensuring replicas are placed in different failure domains to prevent correlated data loss. Changing replication factor after a keyspace is created requires running nodetool repair to redistribute data to new replicas, making initial keyspace design decisions significant and difficult to reverse.

children.title: nodetool and Basic Operations
children.description: The nodetool CLI is Cassandra's primary operational interface, exposing commands for viewing ring status, triggering compaction, running repairs, and draining nodes before maintenance—skills every engineer who operates Cassandra must have. nodetool status provides a quick overview of cluster health showing node states (UN=Up/Normal, DN=Down/Normal), load distribution, and token ranges that diagnose uneven data distribution. Regular nodetool repair is essential because Cassandra does not automatically heal replicas that diverged during a node outage, and skipping repairs leads to stale data returning after read repair fails.

children.title: Memtable, SSTable, and Commit Log
children.description: Cassandra writes go first to the commit log for durability, then to the in-memory memtable; when the memtable fills it is flushed to an immutable SSTable on disk. This write path makes Cassandra extremely fast for writes—it never updates data in place—but accumulates SSTables over time that must be merged (compacted) to remove tombstones and superseded versions. Understanding this lifecycle prevents confusion when a DELETE produces a tombstone that slows reads before compaction removes it, which is a particularly sharp edge for workloads with high delete rates.

children.title: Read Repair and Hinted Handoff
children.description: When a Cassandra read at QUORUM detects that replicas return different values, it triggers a read repair to bring the minority replica up to date—this is one of the mechanisms by which eventual consistency actually resolves to a consistent state. Hinted handoff stores writes locally when a target node is down and replays them when that node recovers, ensuring a brief node outage does not produce permanent data gaps. Both mechanisms operate automatically but can produce temporary latency spikes, so monitoring replica divergence rates and hint replay queue sizes reveals whether repairs are keeping up with mutations.

resources.label: Cassandra Read Repair and Hinted Handoff

nosql-engineer.title: DynamoDB Design and Operations
nosql-engineer.description: Design DynamoDB tables using single-table design principles for efficiency and scalability. Use Global Secondary Indexes to support multiple access patterns.
nosql-engineer.challenge: Design a DynamoDB single-table schema for an order management system that supports lookups by order ID, customer orders list, and orders by status.

children.title: Partition Key and Sort Key Selection
children.description: DynamoDB distributes data across partitions by hashing the partition key, so a poorly chosen key—such as a status enum with only three values—concentrates all traffic on a few partitions and creates hot spots that throttle the entire table. The sort key enables range queries and hierarchical relationships within a partition, making the PK+SK pair the primary design lever for supporting multiple access patterns without additional indexes. Single-table design relies on using composite keys with prefixed values to store multiple entity types and relationships in one table with predictable performance.

resources.label: DynamoDB Single Table Design – Rick Houlihan

children.title: Single-Table Design Patterns
children.description: Single-table design packs all entity types into one DynamoDB table with generic PK/SK attributes, using item type prefixes to distinguish entities and create relationships that map directly to API access patterns. This approach eliminates cross-table joins and reduces round trips because all data for a request can be fetched in one Query operation using begins_with on the sort key. The design is harder to evolve because adding a new access pattern may require a new GSI and a data migration to populate it.

children.title: Global Secondary Indexes (GSI)
children.description: A GSI projects selected attributes onto a new index with a different partition key and optional sort key, effectively creating an alternative view of the table that supports access patterns the base table's key structure cannot serve efficiently. GSIs are eventually consistent and have their own provisioned or on-demand capacity, so a GSI that receives disproportionate write traffic can throttle even when the base table has headroom. Planning the right set of GSIs upfront—aligned to your entity access matrix—prevents the expensive retroactive addition of indexes that requires backfilling all existing items.

children.title: Local Secondary Indexes (LSI)
children.description: LSIs must be created at table creation time and share the base table's partition key while offering an alternative sort key, making them suitable for queries that need to sort or filter on a different attribute within the same partition without the eventual consistency of a GSI. Because LSIs share partition capacity with the base table, a heavy LSI read does not starve the primary access pattern—but LSIs are capped at 10 GB per partition key value, limiting their usefulness for high-volume entities. In most single-table designs GSIs are preferred over LSIs because their flexibility outweighs the slight consistency advantage.

children.title: On-Demand vs Provisioned Capacity
children.description: On-demand capacity mode automatically scales read and write capacity in response to traffic spikes without capacity planning, making it ideal for unpredictable workloads or new tables where usage patterns are unknown. Provisioned capacity mode with auto-scaling is significantly cheaper for predictable, steady-state workloads—often 50-70% less expensive—because you commit to a baseline that auto-scaling adjusts within configured bounds. Switching between modes is allowed twice per day, so teams can start on-demand during early development and migrate to provisioned once usage patterns stabilize.

children.title: DynamoDB Streams and Event-Driven Patterns
children.description: DynamoDB Streams capture a time-ordered sequence of item-level changes (INSERT, MODIFY, REMOVE) and retain them for 24 hours, enabling Lambda functions or Kinesis consumers to react to data changes without polling. This event-driven pattern is widely used to invalidate downstream caches, trigger notifications, replicate data to Elasticsearch for search, and implement audit trails without coupling the write path to these side effects. The stream record includes both old and new item images when configured, making it straightforward to compute diffs and emit only meaningful changes to downstream consumers.

children.title: DynamoDB Transactions
children.description: DynamoDB TransactWriteItems and TransactGetItems provide all-or-nothing semantics across up to 100 items in a single request, enabling atomic multi-item operations that were previously impossible without application-level coordination. Transactions carry twice the cost of individual operations and are not suitable for high-throughput hot paths, but they are the correct tool for financial debit/credit patterns and inventory reservations where partial success would corrupt domain invariants. Condition expressions within transactions let you implement optimistic locking by failing the entire transaction if a version attribute has changed since the item was last read.

children.title: Condition Expressions and Optimistic Locking
children.description: Condition expressions in DynamoDB allow writes to succeed only when specified attributes meet defined criteria—preventing overwriting an item that was modified by another process between read and write. Implementing a version counter attribute that is incremented on every write and checked as a condition enables optimistic concurrency control, the correct pattern for DynamoDB because pessimistic locking has no built-in primitive. This prevents lost updates in concurrent write scenarios, and the ConditionalCheckFailedException surfaces the conflict to the application for retry logic.

nosql-engineer.title: Redis Advanced Patterns
nosql-engineer.description: Use advanced Redis features including Pub/Sub messaging, Redis Streams for event processing, and RedisSearch for full-text search capabilities.
nosql-engineer.challenge: Implement a real-time activity feed using Redis Streams with consumer groups, and add a distributed rate limiter for an API endpoint.

children.title: Redis Pub/Sub
children.description: Redis Pub/Sub implements a fire-and-forget broadcast model where publishers emit messages to channels and all currently subscribed clients receive them, with no persistence—messages are dropped if no subscriber is listening or if a subscriber is temporarily disconnected. This makes Pub/Sub suitable for real-time notifications, live dashboard updates, and chat presence signals where missing a message is acceptable and low latency is paramount. For any use case requiring guaranteed delivery, message replay after reconnection, or consumer acknowledgement, Redis Streams is the correct replacement.

children.title: Redis Streams and Consumer Groups
children.description: Redis Streams provide a persistent, append-only log with message IDs based on timestamp, enabling multiple consumer groups to read the same stream independently and track their position using acknowledged IDs. Consumer groups ensure each message in a stream is delivered to exactly one consumer within the group—providing at-least-once delivery semantics—while the XPENDING command shows messages that were delivered but not yet acknowledged. This makes Redis Streams a lightweight alternative to Kafka for event-sourcing and task queue use cases where Redis is already in the stack.

children.title: Sliding Window Rate Limiting with Sorted Sets
children.description: A sliding window rate limiter uses a Redis Sorted Set keyed by user ID where each request adds the current timestamp as both score and member, and the limiter queries the count of members in the last N seconds using ZRANGEBYSCORE before deciding to allow or reject. This approach is more accurate than a fixed window counter because it does not suffer from the boundary burst problem—where a user can send double the limit by front-loading requests at the end of one window and the start of the next. Trimming old entries with ZREMRANGEBYSCORE on each request and setting a TTL on the key ensures the set does not grow indefinitely.

resources.label: Redis Rate Limiting – Sorted Set Approach

children.title: Distributed Locks with Redlock
children.description: The Redlock algorithm implements a distributed lock across multiple independent Redis nodes, where a lock is considered acquired only when a majority of nodes successfully SET the lock key with a unique token and TTL—preventing a single node failure from making the lock permanently unavailable or allowing two clients to hold it simultaneously. The TTL must be set to a value longer than the expected critical section duration but short enough to release automatically if the holder crashes before explicit unlock. Redlock is suitable for leader election and distributed task deduplication, but for scenarios requiring strict linearizable guarantees a fencing token approach should be added.

resources.label: Redis Distributed Locking – How it Works

children.title: RedisSearch Full-Text Indexing
children.description: RedisSearch (now part of Redis Stack) adds inverted index structures to Redis hashes and JSON documents, enabling full-text search, numeric range filters, and geo-distance queries at in-memory speeds without a separate Elasticsearch cluster. Creating an index with FT.CREATE specifies which fields to index and their types—TEXT with stemming for search fields, NUMERIC for range queries, TAG for exact facet filters—and FT.SEARCH queries the index with a familiar query syntax supporting boolean operators. For applications already using Redis where search is a secondary concern, RedisSearch eliminates the operational overhead of maintaining a separate search engine at the cost of Redis memory holding the index.

children.title: Redis JSON Module
children.description: RedisJSON stores, updates, and queries JSON documents natively using JSONPath expressions, enabling partial updates of deeply nested fields without serializing and deserializing the entire document in the application. JSON.SET creates or replaces a document or a path within it, while JSON.GET with a JSONPath expression extracts only the needed fields—reducing network payload for large documents. Combined with RedisSearch, RedisJSON enables indexing specific JSON fields for search while keeping the full document retrievable in one round trip.

children.title: Redis Sentinel for HA
children.description: Redis Sentinel monitors a primary-replica setup and automatically promotes a replica to primary when the primary fails, updating clients with the new primary address to minimize downtime without manual intervention. Sentinel requires a quorum of Sentinel nodes to agree a primary is down before triggering failover, preventing split-brain promotions during network partitions. Clients must be Sentinel-aware and query Sentinel for the current primary address on startup and on connection errors—using a plain connection string that hardcodes the primary IP breaks transparently during failover.

children.title: Redis Cluster for Horizontal Sharding
children.description: Redis Cluster automatically shards data across up to 1000 nodes by assigning each key to one of 16384 hash slots, enabling horizontal scale for workloads that exceed single-node memory capacity. Multi-key operations are restricted to keys that hash to the same slot unless hash tags {} are used to force co-location. Planning hash tag usage for related keys—such as {user:123}:cart and {user:123}:session—ensures they land on the same node and can be used in MULTI/EXEC transactions and Lua scripts together.

nosql-engineer.title: Cassandra Data Modeling Patterns
nosql-engineer.description: Apply advanced Cassandra data modeling patterns including time-series, user activity, and time-bucket patterns. Handle multi-access-pattern requirements with denormalization.
nosql-engineer.challenge: Re-design the event log schema from the earlier layer to handle a traffic spike scenario using time-bucket partitioning and test it with a load test.

children.title: Time-Bucket Partitioning
children.description: Storing all events for a device or user in a single partition—using a plain ID as the partition key—creates an unbounded partition that grows forever and eventually causes compaction and read performance problems as millions of rows accumulate. The time-bucket pattern solves this by incorporating a time period (hour, day, week) into the partition key, capping partition size while still allowing efficient range queries within a bucket. Applications must query multiple partitions across bucket boundaries in parallel and merge results, but this scatter-gather is fast because each bucket partition is small and resides on a known set of nodes.

children.title: Tombstones and Compaction Management
children.description: Every DELETE in Cassandra writes a tombstone marker rather than removing the data immediately, because the deleted row may still exist on replicas that did not receive the delete—tombstones are how Cassandra propagates deletes across the ring. Tombstones accumulate until compaction runs and are read alongside live rows during queries, causing read latency to increase proportionally to the number of tombstones encountered per partition. Workloads with high delete rates must use TTL-based expiry instead of explicit deletes to limit tombstone accumulation.

resources.label: Cassandra Tombstones and Compaction Deep Dive

children.title: Materialized Views in Cassandra
children.description: Cassandra Materialized Views automatically maintain a denormalized copy of a base table with a different primary key to support a different query pattern, and Cassandra handles keeping the view in sync with base table writes. However, MV updates are applied asynchronously which means a read of the MV immediately after a base table write may return stale data, and view updates add latency to base table writes. For most production workloads, teams prefer manually managing denormalized tables because it gives explicit control over the write fan-out and avoids MV's experimental stability history.

resources.label: Cassandra Materialized Views – Pros and Cons

children.title: Secondary Index Limitations
children.description: Cassandra secondary indexes add a local index on each node that maps non-primary-key values to partition keys, but querying one requires contacting every node in the cluster to check its local index—making them only suitable for low-cardinality columns queried alongside a partition key restriction. Using a secondary index on a high-cardinality column like email address without a partition key restriction causes a full cluster scan equivalent to ALLOW FILTERING, which is functionally unusable in production. The safe alternative is a dedicated lookup table where the indexed column becomes the partition key.

resources.label: Cassandra Secondary Indexes – When to Use

children.title: Denormalization and Lookup Tables
children.description: Cassandra requires data to be duplicated across multiple tables whenever the same entity must be queried by different keys—a user must be stored in a by-email table and a by-id table to support both lookup patterns. While this feels redundant coming from a relational background, the cost of maintaining dual writes is vastly outweighed by the performance benefit of partition-local reads that do not require cluster-wide coordination. Application-level dual-write code must be designed defensively with retry logic and idempotent inserts to handle the partial-failure scenario where one write succeeds and the other does not.

children.title: Batch Operations and Anti-patterns
children.description: Logged batches in Cassandra guarantee that all statements in the batch are either all applied or rolled back by writing a batch log to a coordinator before replay, but they are not a performance optimization—batches with statements spanning multiple partitions still require the coordinator to contact each partition separately. Unlogged batches provide no atomicity guarantee and should only be used for multi-partition writes where performance is critical and partial failure is acceptable. The common mistake of using batches to simulate SQL bulk inserts causes coordinator overload and write amplification, which is the exact opposite of the intended performance gain.

children.title: Partition Size Estimation
children.description: Cassandra performs best when partitions are kept under 100 MB and ideally under 10 MB, because large partitions require more memory during compaction, slow range reads, and cannot be split across nodes after creation. Estimating partition size requires knowing the number of rows per partition key, the average row size, and the compaction strategy's amplification factor. Building a partition sizing spreadsheet before writing the first byte of schema is a discipline that prevents the most common Cassandra scalability problems encountered after months of data accumulation.

children.title: Compaction Strategy Selection
children.description: Cassandra's compaction strategy determines how SSTables are merged and significantly affects read latency, write amplification, and space amplification for different workload patterns. SizeTieredCompactionStrategy (STCS) is optimized for write-heavy workloads, LeveledCompactionStrategy (LCS) limits read amplification—ideal for read-heavy workloads—at the cost of higher write amplification, and TimeWindowCompactionStrategy (TWCS) is purpose-built for time-series data. TWCS must be used with time-bucket schemas to prevent excessive SSTable proliferation when old data is no longer written.

resources.label: Choosing the Right Cassandra Compaction Strategy

nosql-engineer.title: Multi-model and Polyglot Persistence
nosql-engineer.description: Design a polyglot persistence architecture where different data stores serve different components based on their strengths. Manage consistency across multiple stores.
nosql-engineer.challenge: Design an e-commerce system using PostgreSQL for orders, Redis for cart and sessions, and Elasticsearch for product search, with an event-driven sync layer.

children.title: CQRS with Multiple Data Stores
children.description: Command Query Responsibility Segregation separates write commands from read queries, enabling each side to use the data store optimized for its workload—writes go to a transactional SQL database for correctness while reads are served from a denormalized read model in MongoDB or a search index in Elasticsearch. This pattern scales read-heavy systems without scaling the write database and allows the read model to be rebuilt from the event log when requirements change, making schema evolution less costly. Teams should only adopt CQRS when simpler read replicas or caching no longer provide sufficient read scalability.

children.title: Saga Pattern for Distributed Transactions
children.description: In a polyglot persistence architecture, a single business operation may span multiple databases, and the Saga pattern manages this by breaking the operation into a sequence of local transactions each publishing an event, with compensating transactions defined for each step to undo changes if a later step fails. Choreography-based sagas use event-driven coordination where each service reacts to events from others, while orchestration-based sagas use a central coordinator that explicitly invokes each step. Choosing between these approaches is a trade-off between coupling and observability.

children.title: Event-Driven Sync Between Stores
children.description: Keeping multiple data stores synchronized requires an event-driven pipeline where writes to the primary store emit domain events consumed by secondary stores asynchronously, avoiding direct coupling between services. Change Data Capture tools like Debezium read the database transaction log and publish row-level changes to Kafka without requiring application code changes, enabling sync without dual-write logic scattered across service layers. The key design challenge is handling consumer lag—secondary stores may be temporarily behind the primary—and designing APIs that tolerate or surface this eventual consistency.

resources.label: Change Data Capture with Debezium and Kafka

children.title: Read Model Caching Strategies
children.description: Read models in a polyglot architecture are often cached in Redis to avoid hitting Elasticsearch or MongoDB on every request, and the cache invalidation strategy must match the consistency requirements of the view. Write-through invalidation emits a cache delete event alongside every domain event, keeping the cache fresh without polling. Projecting domain events into pre-computed read model documents and caching them by view ID is more efficient than caching raw database query results, because view documents are shaped for the UI and do not require further transformation at read time.

children.title: Matching Store to Workload
children.description: A structured polyglot decision process maps each data domain to the store with the best fit: transactional user accounts and financial records go to PostgreSQL for ACID guarantees, product catalogs and flexible-schema content go to MongoDB, session state and caching go to Redis, search and faceting go to Elasticsearch, and time-series telemetry goes to a purpose-built store. Resist standardizing on one database for all domains, but also resist using seven databases when three would suffice, since each additional system adds operational toil. Documenting the decision rationale for each database in an architecture decision record prevents later engineers from questioning or replacing stores without understanding the original constraints.

children.title: Operational Complexity Trade-offs
children.description: Each additional database in a polyglot architecture introduces its own operational surface: backup procedures, version upgrades, monitoring exporters, access control policies, connection pool management, and on-call knowledge. Teams frequently underestimate this cost during the design phase and only encounter it when the first database upgrade breaks an integration or the on-call rotation lacks expertise in the newer system. A practical rule is that each database must provide a clear order-of-magnitude benefit in the dimension that matters—latency, scalability, expressiveness—before its operational cost is justified.

children.title: Eventual Consistency Tolerance in UI
children.description: When a write goes to a primary store and propagates asynchronously to secondary stores, the UI must be designed to tolerate the propagation lag or implement read-your-writes compensation—such as returning the just-submitted data from the command response rather than re-reading from the eventually consistent read model. Showing a 'processing' state and polling for completion is a common pattern for long-running sagas where the UI must not assume the write has propagated. Communicating consistency semantics to product stakeholders early prevents the expectation that a search index update will be instant when it is by design asynchronous.

resources.label: Read Your Own Writes Consistency – AWS DynamoDB

children.title: Outbox Pattern for Reliable Event Publishing
children.description: The outbox pattern solves the dual-write problem in event-driven polyglot systems: instead of writing to a database and publishing to a message broker in two separate operations that can fail independently, the event is written to an outbox table within the same database transaction as the domain change. A separate process reads the outbox table and publishes the event to the broker, guaranteeing that no event is lost even if the broker is temporarily unavailable. This pattern is the production-grade solution to the at-least-once delivery requirement and eliminates the class of bugs where a service writes data but the downstream event silently disappears.

nosql-engineer.title: Observability and Performance Tuning
nosql-engineer.description: Monitor and tune multiple NoSQL systems using metrics, tracing, and profiling tools. Set up dashboards and alerts for key performance indicators.
topics: Redis INFO command and latency tracking
nosql-engineer.challenge: Set up Prometheus exporters and Grafana dashboards for MongoDB, Redis, and Cassandra running locally, and create alerts for high latency and error rate.

children.title: Prometheus Exporters for NoSQL Systems
children.description: Prometheus scrapes time-series metrics from exporters—dedicated processes that translate database-native metrics into the Prometheus exposition format. Each database requires its own exporter: mongodb_exporter, redis_exporter, and JMX Exporter for Cassandra's JVM metrics, and each must be configured to scrape only the metrics needed to avoid cardinality explosion. Deploying exporters as sidecar containers in Kubernetes alongside database pods allows Prometheus to discover them via annotations without hardcoding scrape targets.

children.title: Key Metrics for Each NoSQL System
children.description: Each NoSQL system has a handful of metrics that serve as leading indicators of performance problems: MongoDB tracks op latency, index miss ratio, and replication lag; Redis tracks hit rate (keyspace_hits / keyspace_misses), connected clients, and memory fragmentation ratio; Cassandra tracks pending compactions, read/write latency percentiles (p99), and pending hints. Alerting on these leading indicators—rather than waiting for user complaints—allows proactive intervention before a metric degrades to the point of user impact. Knowing which dashboard panel to check first for each database reduces mean time to diagnose during incidents.

children.title: MongoDB Query Profiler and explain()
children.description: MongoDB's database profiler captures queries slower than a threshold into the system.profile collection, providing execution stats including documents examined, keys examined, and execution time—the raw material for identifying and fixing slow queries. The explain() method on any query or aggregation returns the query plan chosen by the optimizer, showing whether an index was used (IXSCAN) or a collection scan occurred (COLLSCAN), along with the number of documents examined versus returned. A high examine-to-return ratio is the signature of a missing or mismatched index and the first thing to investigate during a performance regression.

children.title: Cassandra nodetool and CQL Tracing
children.description: Cassandra's nodetool tpstats shows thread pool saturation and pending task queues, with a backlog in ReadStage indicating read throughput has exceeded processing capacity. Enabling query tracing with TRACING ON in cqlsh for a specific query produces a per-operation breakdown of how long each stage took—partition lookup, SSTable reads, merging—making it possible to distinguish between schema problems and hardware problems. The nodetool tablehistograms command provides latency percentiles and SSTable count per table, which together diagnose whether read latency is caused by tombstone accumulation or compaction strategy selection.

children.title: Building NoSQL Dashboards in Grafana
children.description: Grafana community dashboards for MongoDB, Redis, and Cassandra provide a starting template of panels covering the most important metrics, which can be imported in minutes rather than built from scratch. Customizing these dashboards to add SLO thresholds as reference lines and annotation markers for deployments transforms them from monitoring dashboards into incident investigation tools. Organizing dashboard rows by layer—cluster health, per-node vitals, query performance, application-level error rates—lets an on-call engineer scan from top to bottom to isolate whether a problem is infrastructure or query related.

children.title: Alerting on SLO-Aligned Metrics
children.description: Database alerts should be SLO-aligned—alerting on user-impacting symptoms (query p99 latency above threshold, error rate above 1%) rather than on causes (CPU above 80%)—because cause-based alerts frequently fire without impacting users and train on-call engineers to ignore them. Prometheus alerting rules with multi-window burn rate calculations detect SLO budget burn faster than simple threshold alerts and significantly reduce false positives. Pairing each alert with a runbook that links to the relevant Grafana panels, common causes, and remediation commands dramatically reduces time-to-resolve.

children.title: Redis Latency Tracking and Memory Profiling
children.description: Redis LATENCY HISTORY and LATENCY LATEST commands expose spikes in command execution, event loop blocked time, and fork latency during RDB snapshots, making it possible to correlate user-facing latency spikes with specific Redis internal events. The DEBUG SLEEP command in a staging environment simulates a blocked event loop to test how the application handles Redis timeouts, which is preferable to discovering the behavior during a production incident. MEMORY USAGE key and MEMORY DOCTOR provide per-key memory cost and overall memory health analysis, essential for identifying unexpectedly large keys that reduce cache hit rate.

children.title: Connection Pooling and Client-Side Tuning
children.description: Each database connection consumes memory and file descriptors on the server, and without connection pooling, a Node.js or Python application under load can exhaust server connection limits—causing connection refused errors that are misdiagnosed as database failures. Configuring the pool size to match the database's max connection limit prevents exhaustion while ensuring connections are reused efficiently. Monitoring the pool wait time reveals whether the bottleneck is the database or the connection pool size, a distinction that determines whether to scale the database or tune the pool.

nosql-engineer.title: Production Deployment and Resilience
nosql-engineer.description: Deploy NoSQL systems to Kubernetes using Helm charts and manage infrastructure with Terraform. Apply chaos engineering to test resilience under failure conditions.
topics: Helm charts for MongoDB, Redis, Cassandra
nosql-engineer.challenge: Deploy a MongoDB replica set and Redis cluster on Kubernetes using Helm, inject pod failures with a chaos tool, and verify the system recovers automatically.

children.title: StatefulSets for Stateful Workloads
children.description: Kubernetes StatefulSets provide stable network identities (pod-0, pod-1) and persistent volume claims that survive pod restarts—both essential for databases that rely on stable hostnames for cluster membership and that must not lose data when a pod is rescheduled to a different node. Unlike Deployments, StatefulSets update pods sequentially and in order, allowing databases like Cassandra to drain a node before the next one restarts, preserving cluster health during rolling upgrades. Misconfiguring storage class, persistent volume reclaim policy, or affinity rules is the most common cause of data loss in Kubernetes-hosted databases.

children.title: Helm Charts for NoSQL Databases
children.description: Official Helm charts from Bitnami and database vendors encapsulate the StatefulSet, Services, ConfigMaps, and PodDisruptionBudgets needed for a production-grade database deployment, dramatically reducing the Kubernetes manifest complexity teams must maintain. Overriding chart values to set resource requests and limits, replica count, storage class, and anti-affinity rules is safer than forking and modifying chart templates, because chart upgrades then only require updating the values file. Pinning the chart version in your GitOps repository and using a separate values file per environment provides the reproducibility needed for disaster recovery scenarios.

resources.label: Bitnami Helm Charts – MongoDB, Redis, Cassandra
Helm Charts for Databases on Kubernetes

children.title: Terraform for Managed Cloud NoSQL
children.description: Terraform providers for AWS (DynamoDB, DocumentDB, ElastiCache), GCP (Firestore, Bigtable, Memorystore), and Azure (CosmosDB, Azure Cache for Redis) allow declaring managed NoSQL infrastructure as version-controlled code that is reproducibly deployed across environments. Storing Terraform state in a remote backend with state locking prevents concurrent apply operations from corrupting infrastructure state and enables collaboration between team members. Separating database infrastructure into its own Terraform module with explicit outputs for connection strings and endpoints allows application infrastructure to depend on the database module without tightly coupling their lifecycle.

children.title: PersistentVolumeClaim Management
children.description: PersistentVolumeClaims in Kubernetes bind to storage class-provisioned volumes and must be correctly sized, using the right access mode (ReadWriteOnce for single-pod databases like Redis primary) and reclaim policy to prevent accidental deletion when a StatefulSet is scaled down. The reclaim policy must be set to Retain for production databases so that deleting a PVC does not immediately delete the underlying volume, preserving data for manual recovery after an accidental scale-down. Implementing regular volume snapshots using VolumeSnapshot resources provides point-in-time backup capability that works alongside database-native backups.

resources.label: Kubernetes Storage for Databases – Best Practices

children.title: Chaos Engineering for Databases
children.description: Chaos engineering for NoSQL systems validates that the system recovers automatically from node failures, network partitions, and disk pressure—failure modes that are inevitable in production but rarely tested before they occur. Tools like Chaos Mesh and LitmusChaos inject pod kills, network latency, CPU stress, and I/O errors into a Kubernetes cluster in a controlled manner, allowing you to observe exactly how the database cluster and application behave under each failure type. Running chaos experiments in a staging environment that closely mirrors production is the responsible way to build confidence in resilience before it is needed.

children.title: Disaster Recovery and Backup Strategy
children.description: A disaster recovery strategy for NoSQL systems must define RPO (how much data loss is acceptable) and RTO (how long recovery can take), because these targets determine the required backup frequency and the automation needed for failover. For MongoDB this typically means replica set promotion for single-node failures (RTO minutes, RPO near-zero) and Atlas backup or mongodump for corruption recovery (RTO hours, RPO hours). Regularly testing restores in a non-production environment is the only way to discover backup failures or procedural gaps before a real disaster reveals them.

children.title: PodDisruptionBudgets and Rolling Upgrades
children.description: A PodDisruptionBudget (PDB) limits how many pods in a StatefulSet Kubernetes is allowed to terminate simultaneously during voluntary disruptions like node drains and cluster upgrades, ensuring a quorum of database replicas remains available throughout. Without a PDB, a node drain during a cluster upgrade can evict all pods of a three-replica Cassandra cluster simultaneously, causing a complete database outage. Configuring minAvailable or maxUnavailable in the PDB to match the database's minimum viable replica count—and validating that the PDB actually blocks eviction by simulating a node drain in staging—is a non-negotiable production readiness check.

children.title: Security Hardening for Production NoSQL
children.description: Production NoSQL deployments must enforce authentication (disabled by default in many self-hosted instances), encrypt data at rest and in transit using TLS, and restrict network access to application pods using Kubernetes NetworkPolicies that explicitly allowlist which services can reach each database port. Role-based access control within MongoDB, Cassandra, and DynamoDB should follow the principle of least privilege—application service accounts should have read/write on specific collections but no ability to drop databases or modify schema. Secrets for database credentials must never be hardcoded or stored in plaintext ConfigMaps; instead they should be managed by Kubernetes Secrets with envelope encryption or an external secrets manager.

db-reliability.title: Database Reliability Fundamentals
db-reliability.description: Understand SRE principles applied to database systems, including SLOs, error budgets, and the reliability mindset. Learn how databases fail and how to reason about reliability.
db-reliability.challenge: Define SLOs for a hypothetical production database, including availability, query latency p99, and replication lag, with justification for each target.

children.title: SLOs and Error Budgets
children.description: Service Level Objectives give database teams a shared, quantitative contract with product teams about acceptable reliability. Error budgets translate that contract into a concrete allowance for risk—when the budget is healthy you ship faster, when it's tight you slow down and stabilize. Without this framework, reliability debates remain subjective and politically charged.

resources.label: Google SRE Book – Service Level Objectives
SLOs, SLAs, and Error Budgets Explained

children.title: Common Database Failure Modes
children.description: Databases fail in patterns that repeat across organizations—disk exhaustion, connection pool saturation, replication lag spirals, and long-running transactions causing lock storms. Cataloging these failure modes in advance lets teams build targeted detection and mitigation before incidents strike. Knowing the blast radius of each failure mode also helps prioritize which to address first.

resources.label: Common PostgreSQL Failure Modes and Mitigations

children.title: Metrics, Logs, and Traces
children.description: The three pillars of observability serve distinct but complementary roles for database systems: metrics reveal trends and thresholds, logs surface the exact SQL and errors at failure time, and distributed traces connect slow database calls to the upstream application context. Building all three from the start avoids the painful situation of having an alert fire with no actionable diagnostic data. Each pillar has different retention and cardinality trade-offs that drive tooling choices.

children.title: Toil Identification and Reduction
children.description: Toil—manual, repetitive operational work—is the silent killer of database reliability teams because it crowds out the project work that would prevent the toil in the first place. SRE doctrine limits toil to 50% of an engineer's time, and tracking it exposes which manual tasks should be automated or eliminated. For databases, common sources are manual failovers, slow-query triage, and certificate renewals.

resources.label: How to Measure and Reduce Toil

children.title: Severity Classification for Database Incidents
children.description: Classifying database incidents by severity ensures the right people are paged at the right time and that response urgency is proportional to user impact. A severity matrix that maps database symptoms (replica down, primary down, disk at 90%) to severity levels removes ambiguous judgment calls during high-stress moments. Clear severity definitions also feed into MTTR calculations and postmortem prioritization.

children.title: Blameless Postmortem Culture
children.description: Blameless postmortems treat database outages as systems failures rather than individual failures, which encourages engineers to share complete and honest timelines without fear of punishment. This psychological safety is the mechanism by which organizations actually learn from incidents and prevent recurrence. Postmortems without blameless culture produce sanitized write-ups that protect people but teach nothing.

resources.label: How to Run a Blameless Postmortem

children.title: Reliability as a Feature
children.description: Treating database reliability as a product feature—with its own roadmap, backlog, and success metrics—elevates it from reactive firefighting to proactive engineering. Teams that invest in reliability tooling and runbooks before failures occur consistently achieve lower MTTR than teams that treat reliability as a background concern. This framing also makes reliability work legible to product managers and executives who control engineering resources.

resources.label: Making Reliability a Feature – Google Cloud Blog
SRE Fundamentals – Google Cloud Skills Boost

children.title: MTTR vs MTBF Trade-offs
children.description: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR) pull engineering investment in different directions: MTBF optimization prevents incidents through redundancy and testing, while MTTR optimization speeds recovery through automation and runbooks. For databases, improving MTTR often delivers faster reliability gains than chasing MTBF because some failure modes cannot be fully prevented. Knowing which metric to optimize for a given system requires understanding the cost of downtime versus the cost of prevention.

resources.label: MTBF vs MTTR: Which Should You Optimize?

db-reliability.title: Monitoring and Alerting
db-reliability.description: Set up a full monitoring stack for database systems using Prometheus, Grafana, and exporters. Create actionable alerts based on golden signals.
db-reliability.challenge: Deploy Prometheus with postgresql_exporter and mysqld_exporter, create Grafana dashboards for each, and write alert rules for connection saturation and replication lag.

children.title: Database-Specific Exporters
children.description: Database exporters act as translators between a database's native metrics interface and Prometheus's pull-based scrape model. Using the correct exporter (postgres_exporter, mysqld_exporter, redis_exporter) ensures you capture the semantics of each database system rather than generic process metrics. Exporter configuration also governs which internal views and statements are queried, directly affecting the completeness of your observability.

children.title: PromQL for Database Alerting
children.description: PromQL's time-series query model is ideally suited to expressing database alert conditions like sustained replication lag, connection pool near-saturation, or query rate anomalies over a rolling window. Writing alert expressions as rate-based rather than instantaneous values prevents alert flapping during brief spikes. Mastering PromQL functions like rate(), increase(), and histogram_quantile() unlocks precise SLO burn-rate alerts.

children.title: Golden Signals Dashboard
children.description: The four golden signals—latency, traffic, errors, and saturation—provide a minimal but complete lens through which to evaluate any database system's health at a glance. Organizing a Grafana dashboard around these signals instead of raw metrics makes dashboards immediately interpretable during incidents, even for engineers unfamiliar with the specific database. Consistent golden-signal dashboards across all database types also reduce the cognitive load during multi-system incidents.

resources.label: Building a Database Monitoring Dashboard in Grafana

children.title: Pre-built Grafana Dashboards
children.description: The Grafana dashboard library contains production-tested community dashboards for every major database system, saving hours of panel construction and encoding institutional knowledge about what metrics actually matter. Starting with a community dashboard and then customizing it is faster than building from scratch and ensures coverage of metrics you might not know to look for. Forking a pinned dashboard version into your own repository also prevents upstream changes from breaking your production views.

children.title: Alert Routing and Grouping
children.description: AlertManager's routing tree determines which team receives which alert, reducing noise by grouping related alerts into a single notification and silencing known-maintenance windows. Poorly configured routing creates alert fatigue, which is one of the primary reasons on-call engineers miss critical signals during actual incidents. Grouping alerts by database cluster and environment ensures that a single node failure doesn't generate dozens of independent pages.

children.title: Alert Fatigue Prevention
children.description: Alert fatigue occurs when on-call engineers receive so many low-signal notifications that they begin ignoring pages, which guarantees that critical incidents are missed. Database teams should audit alert firing rates weekly and ruthlessly delete or raise thresholds on alerts that resolve without human action. Every alert that fires should either represent a genuine threat to SLOs or prompt a human decision that automation cannot make.

resources.label: My Philosophy on Alerting – Rob Ewaschuk
Reducing Alert Fatigue in SRE Teams

children.title: Query Performance Monitoring
children.description: Tracking per-query latency histograms over time reveals query regressions as soon as they are deployed rather than after they generate user complaints. Both pg_stat_statements (PostgreSQL) and the Performance Schema (MySQL) surface normalized query digests with total time, call count, and mean latency—enough to rank the worst offenders and prioritize tuning effort. Shipping these metrics to Prometheus enables alerting on individual query digest latency thresholds.

children.title: Replication Lag Monitoring
children.description: Replication lag is one of the most consequential metrics for read-heavy database clusters because applications routing reads to replicas will see stale data when lag climbs. A spike in replication lag is also an early warning sign of an overloaded primary before that overload manifests as latency for write clients. Monitoring lag with sub-second resolution and alerting at both a warning threshold and a critical threshold gives teams time to reroute traffic before data staleness becomes user-visible.

db-reliability.title: Backup Strategies and Validation
db-reliability.description: Design and implement comprehensive backup strategies for multiple database systems. Validate backups regularly by performing test restores in isolated environments.
db-reliability.challenge: Automate weekly full and nightly incremental backups for a PostgreSQL instance, run a weekly restore test in a separate environment, and alert on restore failure.

children.title: Full vs Incremental Backup Trade-offs
children.description: Full backups provide self-contained restore points but consume significant storage and I/O bandwidth proportional to database size, making them impractical to run frequently. Incremental and differential backups reduce storage costs but introduce restore complexity and increase the window during which a backup chain corruption can leave you with no valid recovery point. A tiered strategy—daily incremental with weekly full—balances these concerns while staying within acceptable RPO windows.

children.title: Physical vs Logical Backups
children.description: Physical backups copy the raw data files and are faster to restore at scale but are tightly coupled to the source database version and file system layout, making cross-version migrations difficult. Logical backups (pg_dump, mysqldump) produce portable SQL that can be restored to different versions or even different database engines, but their restore times grow linearly with data volume. Choosing between them requires knowing both the RPO/RTO requirements and whether version portability is a requirement.

children.title: pgBackRest for PostgreSQL
children.description: pgBackRest is the de facto standard for production PostgreSQL backups because it supports parallelized backup and restore, WAL archiving, incremental backups using block-level change tracking, and built-in encryption and compression. Its retention policy system automatically expires old backups while preserving enough WAL to reach the full retention boundary, preventing both storage overflow and accidental loss of recovery points. Configuration-as-code via pgbackrest.conf makes backup policies auditable in version control.

children.title: Percona XtraBackup for MySQL
children.description: XtraBackup takes hot physical backups of InnoDB databases without acquiring a global read lock, which means it can run during peak production hours without causing write stalls. Its streaming and compression modes reduce the time data spends in transit when shipping backups to object storage, directly compressing the backup window. XtraBackup also supports incremental backups based on InnoDB LSN (log sequence number), enabling efficient daily incremental chains on multi-terabyte databases.

children.title: Automated Restore Testing
children.description: An untested backup is not a backup—it is a hope, and hopes fail precisely during the high-pressure moments when backups are most needed. Automated restore testing in an isolated environment on a scheduled cadence (weekly at minimum) provides a continuous, evidence-based guarantee that the restore path works and that the restored data passes consistency checks. When restore tests fail silently and no human notices, the true backup window is zero.

children.title: Checksum Verification
children.description: Storage corruption, network bit flips, and buggy backup software can all produce backup files that pass superficial size checks but contain silently corrupt data that only becomes apparent during restore. Checksumming every backup file at write time and verifying checksums before restore catches these corruptions before they cause an irrecoverable data loss event. PostgreSQL's data checksums and pgBackRest's built-in checksum verification both provide this safety net at the database level.

children.title: Backup Retention Policies
children.description: Retention policies must satisfy two opposing pressures: compliance requirements that mandate long-term preservation of backups, and storage costs that grow linearly with retention length. A tiered retention policy—keep daily backups for 30 days, weekly backups for 3 months, and monthly backups for 1 year—provides a reasonable baseline for most regulated environments. Automating expiry in the backup tool (pgBackRest's retention-full, XtraBackup's scripted purge) prevents retention creep that silently inflates storage bills.

children.title: Offsite and Cloud Backup Storage
children.description: Storing backups on the same infrastructure as the primary database means a single failure (datacenter fire, ransomware, cloud region outage) can destroy both the primary and all recovery points simultaneously. The 3-2-1 backup rule—three copies, two media types, one offsite—provides defense-in-depth against correlated failures. Cloud object storage (S3, GCS) with versioning and object lock satisfies the offsite and immutability requirements at low cost for most database sizes.

db-reliability.title: Replication Health and Lag Management
db-reliability.description: Monitor and manage replication lag across PostgreSQL and MySQL replica sets. Implement lag-based alerting and automated corrective actions.
db-reliability.challenge: Create a replication lag monitoring script that queries lag from both PostgreSQL and MySQL replication views, logs to a time-series DB, and fires alerts over 30 seconds.

children.title: PostgreSQL Replication Lag Metrics
children.description: PostgreSQL surfaces replication lag through pg_stat_replication on the primary, which provides byte-level write_lag, flush_lag, and replay_lag that reveal exactly where in the replication pipeline a standby is falling behind. Monitoring all three values separately is critical because flush_lag stalling (I/O bound standby) requires a different fix than replay_lag stalling (CPU bound recovery). Converting byte lag to time lag using the primary's WAL generation rate gives a more intuitive alerting metric.

children.title: MySQL GTID-Based Lag Measurement
children.description: SHOW REPLICA STATUS provides Seconds_Behind_Source which is calculated from event timestamps and can be misleadingly zero during periods of no writes even when the replica is far behind in position. A more accurate measurement compares GTID sets between primary and replica to compute the exact transaction gap, which remains non-zero even during quiet periods if the replica is behind. Heartbeat-based lag measurement tools (pt-heartbeat) offer sub-second resolution independent of GTID or binary log format.

resources.label: Accurate MySQL Replication Lag with pt-heartbeat

children.title: I/O and CPU Bottlenecks on Replicas
children.description: Replicas replay WAL or binary log events sequentially (unless parallel replication is configured), which means a replica with slower I/O than the primary will fall progressively further behind during write bursts. Long-running queries on the replica that compete with WAL replay threads for CPU also cause lag spikes even when I/O is sufficient. Profiling replica I/O wait and CPU utilization during lag events is the first step to root-causing the bottleneck.

children.title: Large Transactions and DDL
children.description: A single large transaction that touches millions of rows generates a burst of WAL or binary log events that must be applied atomically on the replica, causing a lag spike that is proportional to the transaction's write volume. DDL statements (ALTER TABLE, CREATE INDEX) are particularly dangerous on MySQL because they hold a replica lock for their entire duration, blocking all subsequent replication during that time. Splitting large writes into batches and using online DDL tools (gh-ost, pt-osc) prevents these lag spikes.

resources.label: Avoiding Replication Lag from Large Batches

children.title: Lag-Based Alert Thresholds
children.description: Setting a single lag alert threshold that works for all applications is impossible because a 10-second lag is catastrophic for a payment system and completely acceptable for a reporting replica. Defining per-consumer lag SLOs based on the staleness tolerance of each downstream application produces meaningful, targeted alerts rather than generic warnings that on-call engineers learn to ignore. Tiered alerts (warning at 30s, critical at 5m) give operators time to investigate before the lag violates SLOs.

children.title: Automatic Lag-Based Read Routing
children.description: Routing read queries to a replica that is significantly behind the primary produces user-visible data inconsistencies that are difficult to diagnose because they are transient and load-dependent. ProxySQL and PgBouncer can be configured to automatically remove high-lag replicas from the read pool and restore them once lag falls below a threshold, providing seamless protection without application-layer awareness. This automation prevents the manual intervention loop where lag causes staleness, staleness causes support tickets, and support tickets cause late-night on-call pages.

resources.label: ProxySQL Read-Write Splitting with Lag Awareness
Lag-Aware Read Routing in Database Proxies

children.title: Semi-Synchronous Replication
children.description: Semi-synchronous replication requires at least one replica to acknowledge receipt of each transaction before the primary commits, which eliminates the data loss window inherent in pure asynchronous replication. This durability improvement comes at a latency cost—each write must wait for a replica network round-trip—which makes it unsuitable for write-heavy workloads with tight latency SLOs unless replicas are co-located. The tradeoff is zero data loss on primary failure versus milliseconds of added commit latency.

children.title: Replication Slots and Lag Accumulation
children.description: PostgreSQL replication slots retain WAL segments until the slowest subscriber has consumed them, which guarantees no data is lost to a lagging replica but can silently fill the primary's disk to 100% if a replica disconnects and the slot is not cleaned up. Monitoring slot lag (pg_replication_slots.confirmed_flush_lsn) and setting a maximum allowed slot lag is essential for preventing disk-full emergencies on the primary. Naming, documenting, and auditing all replication slots regularly prevents orphaned slots from accumulating.

resources.label: Replication Slot Dangers and Best Practices

db-reliability.title: High Availability Architecture
db-reliability.description: Design and deploy high-availability database architectures with automatic failover. Understand the role of VIPs, proxies, and consensus systems in HA setups.
db-reliability.challenge: Deploy Patroni with etcd for a 3-node PostgreSQL cluster, document the failover procedure, and run a chaos test by killing the primary to verify automatic promotion.

children.title: Patroni for PostgreSQL HA
children.description: Patroni implements a distributed leader election on top of a consensus store (etcd, Consul, or ZooKeeper) to ensure that exactly one PostgreSQL node acts as primary at all times, preventing split-brain scenarios that cause divergent writes. Its REST API and patronictl command expose cluster state in a way that is both human-readable during incidents and machine-readable for automation. Patroni's callback hooks enable custom actions on topology changes, such as invalidating application connection pools or updating DNS entries.

children.title: MHA for MySQL High Availability
children.description: MySQL MHA (Master High Availability Manager) monitors the primary and coordinates failover by identifying the most up-to-date replica using binary log positions and promoting it with minimal data loss. Unlike Patroni, MHA does not use a consensus store—it relies on quorum-based SSH connectivity checks—which makes it simpler to deploy but less resistant to network partitions. Understanding MHA's relay log application step during failover is critical because it is the mechanism that closes the gap between primary and replica at the moment of failure.

children.title: ProxySQL for MySQL HA
children.description: ProxySQL sits between application servers and the MySQL cluster, transparently routing writes to the primary and reads to replicas while hiding topology changes from the application layer. During a failover, ProxySQL detects that the old primary is unreachable, waits for the new primary election, and then routes writes to the promoted node—all without requiring application restarts or connection string changes. Its query rule system also enables read-write splitting, query blocking, and query rewriting without application code changes.

children.title: HAProxy and PgBouncer for PostgreSQL HA
children.description: HAProxy provides TCP-level health checking and load balancing in front of PostgreSQL, routing connections based on which node Patroni marks as the primary via a dedicated REST endpoint. PgBouncer adds connection pooling on top, reducing the number of backend connections during traffic spikes and surviving brief primary unavailability during failover by queuing client connections in a pause state. Deploying HAProxy and PgBouncer together gives PostgreSQL clusters the same transparent failover behavior that ProxySQL provides for MySQL.

children.title: Planned Failover Drills
children.description: Planned failover drills—triggering a controlled primary switchover in production during low-traffic hours—validate the entire HA stack under real conditions and build operational muscle memory for the unplanned case. Teams that only test failover in staging discover during real incidents that production network topologies, DNS TTLs, and connection pool behaviors differ in ways that cause failovers to take 3x longer than expected. The goal of a drill is not to prove that failover works but to measure exactly how long it takes and what breaks during the transition.

children.title: Split-Brain Prevention
children.description: Split-brain—two nodes simultaneously believing they are the primary—is the most dangerous failure mode for HA databases because both nodes accept writes that diverge and are impossible to merge automatically. STONITH (Shoot The Other Node In The Head) mechanisms physically power off the suspected old primary during a takeover, providing a hard guarantee against split-brain at the cost of higher RTO. Understanding your HA system's split-brain prevention mechanism and its failure modes is a prerequisite to trusting it in production.

children.title: Application-Level Health Checks
children.description: TCP-level health checks verify that the database port is open but cannot detect scenarios where the database process is running but unable to serve queries—such as a long-running VACUUM FULL holding an exclusive lock. Application-level health checks that execute a lightweight query (SELECT 1) and measure its response time catch these degraded-but-alive states that TCP checks miss. Adding read/write differentiation to health checks—verifying that the primary can write and the replica can read—enables proxies to route correctly even when the database process is partially functional.

children.title: Virtual IP and DNS Failover
children.description: Virtual IP (VIP) failover reassigns a floating IP address from the failed primary to the new primary, allowing applications using that IP to reconnect without configuration changes. However, VIP failover requires that all application servers are in the same L2 broadcast domain as the database nodes, which is increasingly uncommon in cloud environments. DNS-based failover with aggressive TTLs (5-30 seconds) works across L3 boundaries but requires that application DNS resolvers honor short TTLs and that connection pools re-resolve on reconnect.

db-reliability.title: Incident Response for Databases
db-reliability.description: Build incident response processes specific to database outages and degradations. Write runbooks, conduct blameless postmortems, and improve MTTR over time.
topics: Runbook writing for common database incidents
db-reliability.challenge: Write runbooks for three database incidents (replication lag spike, connection pool exhaustion, disk full), and simulate one incident with a teammate using the runbook.

children.title: Writing Effective Database Runbooks
children.description: A database runbook written at 3am by a tired engineer under pressure is useless—runbooks must be written before incidents, tested during drills, and updated after every incident where they were found lacking. Effective runbooks begin with symptoms (what the alert says) rather than root causes (which require diagnosis to establish), because on-call engineers encounter symptoms first. Each runbook step should produce a concrete, verifiable outcome so the responder knows whether the step succeeded before proceeding.

children.title: Runbooks as Code (RaC)
children.description: Storing runbooks in version-controlled Markdown alongside the infrastructure code that generates alerts closes the gap between what an alert fires for and what the runbook instructs the responder to do. Linking alert annotations directly to the relevant runbook section eliminates the 5-minute search for documentation during active incidents. Treating runbooks as code also enables pull request reviews and change history, which catches outdated procedures before they are needed.

resources.label: Prometheus Alertmanager Annotations for Runbook Links

children.title: On-Call Rotation Design
children.description: On-call rotations must balance coverage breadth with engineer health: a rotation that pages a single person for a week generates burnout, while a rotation with too many people on point creates unclear accountability during incidents. Database on-call specifically benefits from a shadow rotation where junior engineers shadow senior responders before taking primary on-call, building competence before responsibility. Scheduled on-call swaps (for vacation, illness, or time zone coverage) need a formal process to prevent coverage gaps.

children.title: Escalation Policies
children.description: Escalation policies define what happens when an initial page is not acknowledged within a timeout—without them, incidents stall when the primary on-call is unavailable or asleep through their phone. For database incidents, escalation typically goes: primary database on-call → senior DBA → database team lead → engineering VP, with each level triggered by a non-acknowledgment timeout. Opsgenie and PagerDuty both implement escalation policies with configurable timeouts and override capabilities for planned gaps.

children.title: Postmortem Template and Timeline
children.description: A structured postmortem template ensures that every incident analysis captures the same set of data: a timeline of events, the detection-to-mitigation gap, contributing causes, and specific action items with owners and due dates. The timeline is the most valuable artifact because it reveals whether the team detected the incident quickly, whether diagnosis was efficient, and whether the mitigation applied was the right one. Gaps in the timeline—periods where responders were confused or waiting—are exactly the places where runbook improvements and tooling investments pay off.

resources.label: How to Conduct a Database Postmortem

children.title: Five Whys for Database Incidents
children.description: The Five Whys technique applied to database incidents drills through proximate causes (the disk was full) to systemic causes (alerts only fire when the disk is 95% full, leaving no time to respond before 100%) to process causes (no one was assigned to act on the 90% warning). Without this depth, postmortem action items address the symptom of the last incident rather than the conditions that make that symptom inevitable. For database incidents, the fifth why often reveals a gap in monitoring, a missing runbook, or an inadequate change management process.

resources.label: Root Cause Analysis for Database Failures

children.title: Automating Common Remediation Steps
children.description: Manual remediation steps that appear in runbooks more than twice are candidates for automation: a script that restarts a stuck replication thread, rotates a full transaction log, or flushes a bloated connection pool removes an entire class of human error from the incident response workflow. Automation also compresses MTTR by orders of magnitude—a manual step that takes 5 minutes to locate in a runbook and execute takes a script 2 seconds. Self-healing automation that monitors for known conditions and applies proven fixes before humans are even paged is the highest leverage investment for database reliability teams.

children.title: Tracking MTTR and MTBF Over Time
children.description: Measuring MTTR and MTBF per database system and per incident type over time provides the empirical basis for prioritizing reliability investments: if connection pool exhaustion has the highest frequency and the longest MTTR, that is where automation and runbook improvement will deliver the most value. These metrics also make reliability work visible to leadership in business terms (incidents per quarter, average downtime per incident) rather than technical terms. Storing incident data in a queryable format (database, spreadsheet, or incident management platform) enables this analysis.

resources.label: Tracking SRE Metrics with Incident Data

db-reliability.title: Capacity Planning and Scaling
db-reliability.description: Forecast database growth using historical metrics and plan capacity additions before thresholds are breached. Decide between vertical scaling, read replicas, and sharding.
topics: Disk, CPU, and connection capacity models
db-reliability.challenge: Use 90 days of Prometheus metrics to build a capacity forecast model, identify when disk will be exhausted, and propose a scaling plan with cost estimate.

children.title: Time-Series Capacity Modeling
children.description: Building a linear or polynomial regression model on 90 days of historical disk usage, QPS, and connection count metrics produces a forward projection that gives teams 30–90 days of lead time before capacity thresholds are breached. Prometheus's predict_linear() function computes this projection directly from stored metrics, enabling dashboard panels that display days until exhaustion without external tooling. Seasonal decomposition (separating weekly traffic cycles from underlying growth) produces more accurate forecasts for workloads with cyclical patterns.

children.title: Resource Utilization Baselines
children.description: Establishing baselines for disk IOPS, CPU utilization, and memory usage during normal load gives capacity planners the reference points needed to identify when a database is approaching its architectural limits rather than just day-to-day peaks. Without baselines, it is impossible to distinguish a new traffic spike from a resource leak or a query regression. Baselines also anchor SLO conversations with product teams—showing the current headroom quantifies how much growth the current infrastructure can absorb before the next scaling event.

children.title: Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling
children.description: Vertical scaling (larger instance class) solves CPU and memory bottlenecks immediately but has a ceiling (the largest available instance) and introduces single-point-of-failure risk unless combined with HA replication. Horizontal scaling (read replicas, sharding) solves throughput limits beyond what any single node can serve but introduces architectural complexity that increases operational burden for years. The correct choice depends on whether the bottleneck is write throughput (where sharding helps) or read throughput (where replicas help) or resource contention (where vertical scaling helps).

children.title: Read Replica Provisioning
children.description: Adding read replicas shifts a portion of read traffic away from the primary, providing both capacity headroom and a secondary site for backup-style operations (reporting, backups, analytics) that would otherwise compete with OLTP workloads. However, replicas are not free: each replica applies WAL at the same rate as the primary's write load, consuming standby I/O and CPU that must be provisioned accordingly. Forecasting when to add replicas based on primary read latency trending toward its SLO threshold prevents reactive scaling decisions that are always too late.

resources.label: AWS RDS Read Replica Best Practices

children.title: Cloud Database Cost Estimation
children.description: Cloud databases (RDS, Cloud SQL, Aurora) charge for instance class, storage, IOPS, data transfer, and backup storage as separate line items, making true cost estimation require a multi-dimensional model rather than a simple instance price lookup. Rightsizing databases—finding the smallest instance class that meets performance SLOs—is often the highest-ROI optimization available, as over-provisioned instances are common in teams without capacity planning practices. AWS Cost Explorer and GCP's billing export provide the historical data needed to build these cost models.

children.title: Storage Growth Cost Projection
children.description: Database storage costs compound over time because historical data is rarely deleted and compression ratios degrade as data patterns diversify. Projecting storage costs 12 months forward and mapping them to quarterly budget cycles allows database teams to negotiate storage infrastructure purchases before emergency procurement becomes necessary. Data archival strategies—moving cold data to cheaper storage tiers (S3, GCS Nearline) or partitioned tables—can reduce storage costs by 60–80% for time-series and event-log workloads.

resources.label: PostgreSQL Table Partitioning for Data Archival

children.title: Disk IOPS and Throughput Capacity
children.description: Database disk performance is constrained by two independent limits—IOPS (transactions per second) and throughput (MB/s)—and workloads hit one limit or the other depending on whether they are I/O small-random or I/O large-sequential. Provisioned IOPS storage in cloud environments allows precise tuning of this limit, but over-provisioning is expensive and under-provisioning causes latency spikes that are difficult to diagnose. Benchmarking with realistic workload patterns using sysbench or pgbench before production deployment establishes the actual IOPS envelope needed.

children.title: Connection Pool Capacity Planning
children.description: PostgreSQL and MySQL have hard limits on concurrent connections (controlled by max_connections) because each connection consumes dedicated memory for query parsing state, authentication context, and transaction management. Applications that do not pool connections can exhaust this limit during traffic spikes, turning every new connection attempt into an error. Sizing connection pools requires modeling peak concurrent request rates in the application tier, not just average rates, because connection exhaustion is a worst-case phenomenon.

db-reliability.title: Database Change Management
db-reliability.description: Manage schema and data changes in production safely using migration tools and online DDL tools. Integrate database migrations into CI/CD pipelines.
db-reliability.challenge: Use gh-ost to add a non-null column with a default to a large MySQL table, integrate the migration into a CI/CD pipeline, and implement an automated rollback trigger.

children.title: gh-ost for MySQL
children.description: gh-ost (GitHub's Online Schema Transmogrifier) performs MySQL DDL changes without holding metadata locks that block writes, by reading the binary log as a change stream and applying it to a ghost table in the background. Unlike pt-online-schema-change, gh-ost does not use triggers which can cause replication lag during high-write migrations. Its cut-over step is atomic and brief (milliseconds), making it the preferred tool for large-table schema changes in production MySQL clusters.

resources.label: gh-ost Deep Dive by GitHub Engineering

children.title: pt-online-schema-change for MySQL
children.description: pt-online-schema-change (pt-osc) from Percona Toolkit creates a shadow table, copies existing rows in chunks, installs triggers to replicate ongoing changes, then performs a RENAME TABLE swap. The trigger-based approach introduces some write amplification that can cause replication lag on busy tables, so chunk size and sleep parameters require tuning to stay within acceptable lag thresholds. Despite gh-ost's advantages, pt-osc remains widely used because it works on older MySQL versions and requires no binary log access.

resources.label: Safe MySQL Schema Changes with pt-osc

children.title: Flyway in CI/CD Pipelines
children.description: Flyway applies versioned SQL migration scripts in sequence, tracking which have been applied in a schema history table, which enables safe parallel deployments because each migration is idempotent after its first application. Integrating Flyway into the CI/CD pipeline before deployment means schema changes are applied automatically and atomically with application code changes, eliminating the manual coordination step that causes version mismatches.
