Flyway's dry-run mode validates migrations against production without applying them, which is essential for review before applying to sensitive databases.

children.title: Backward-Compatible Migration Patterns
children.description: Zero-downtime deployments require that each schema migration be backward-compatible with both the old and new version of the application code, because during a rolling deployment both versions run simultaneously against the same schema. The expand-contract pattern achieves this by splitting breaking changes into three phases: expand (add new structure alongside old), migrate (backfill data), and contract (remove old structure once all traffic is on new code). Skipping the backward-compatibility requirement causes deployment-time failures that require rollback coordination across database and application teams.

children.title: Migration Rollback Planning
children.description: Most database schema changes are difficult or impossible to roll back automatically because DDL cannot be wrapped in a transaction in MySQL and some PostgreSQL DDL holds exclusive locks during execution. Planning rollback before applying a migration—writing the reversal SQL and testing it in staging—ensures that a production regression has a practiced recovery path rather than an improvised one. Feature flags that gate new schema usage at the application level allow schema changes to be deployed days before the application code uses them, decoupling the two rollback concerns.

children.title: Feature Flags for Data Migrations
children.description: Feature flags decouple the deployment of new database schema from the activation of the code paths that use it, which is essential for large data migrations where backfill takes hours or days during which the new columns contain null or partial data. LaunchDarkly, Unleash, and home-grown flag systems all provide the same basic capability: a boolean gate in application code that controls whether the new path executes. Using feature flags for data migrations also enables a gradual rollout—activating the new schema for 1% of traffic first and observing error rates before full activation.

resources.label: Using Feature Flags for Database Migrations

children.title: Schema Change Review Checklist
children.description: A schema change review checklist applied before every production migration catches the most common production-incident causes: missing index on a new foreign key, an ALTER TABLE on a large table without an online DDL tool, a NOT NULL column added without a default, or a migration that lacks a rollback procedure. Automating the checklist as a CI gate prevents these errors from reaching reviewers who may lack the database expertise to spot them independently. The checklist also serves as institutional knowledge documentation for onboarding new database engineers.

children.title: Database Change Advisory Board
children.description: High-risk schema changes—dropping tables, renaming columns, removing constraints on large tables—benefit from a lightweight change advisory board review that brings a second senior database engineer's eyes onto the migration plan before execution. This is not bureaucracy for its own sake: it catches the errors that the migration author is too close to the change to see, the same way code review catches bugs the author already explained away to themselves. The review can be asynchronous (pull request comment) and should target specific risk categories rather than every migration.

resources.label: When Database Changes Need Extra Review

db-reliability.title: Security and Compliance for Databases
db-reliability.description: Implement database security controls required for compliance frameworks including encryption at rest, audit logging, credential rotation, and access reviews.
topics: SOC2 and PCI DSS database controls
db-reliability.challenge: Configure HashiCorp Vault to issue short-lived PostgreSQL credentials, rotate them every 1 hour, and demonstrate that revoked credentials are immediately rejected.

children.title: HashiCorp Vault for Database Credentials
children.description: Vault's database secrets engine generates short-lived, unique database credentials for each application instance, eliminating the long-lived shared passwords that are the root cause of most database credential compromise incidents. When a credential is revoked (application scaled down, deployment rolled back), access is immediately cut without changing any shared password. The audit log Vault produces for every credential issuance and revocation provides the evidence trail required for SOC2 and PCI DSS access reviews.

children.title: Automated Credential Rotation
children.description: Static database passwords that are rotated manually (if ever) become a security liability as the circle of knowledge about them widens over time and departing employees retain access. Automated credential rotation—whether through Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or custom scripts—enforces a maximum credential lifetime that limits the exposure window if credentials are leaked. Application restart coordination during rotation (graceful drain then reconnect) is the primary engineering challenge in rotation automation, not the rotation itself.

sideLeft.title: Encryption at Rest and in Transit

children.title: TLS Configuration for Database Connections
children.description: Unencrypted database connections expose query data and credentials to any observer with network access—a realistic threat in shared-tenant cloud environments and any organization whose network is not perfectly segmented. Enforcing TLS at the database level (ssl=require in PostgreSQL, require_secure_transport=ON in MySQL) ensures that applications cannot accidentally connect unencrypted even when their TLS configuration is misconfigured. Certificate rotation automation is the most common operational challenge with database TLS, as expired certificates cause outages that look like network failures.

children.title: Transparent Data Encryption (TDE)
children.description: Transparent Data Encryption encrypts data files, WAL, and temporary files at the OS level so that physical access to a storage volume (stolen disk, cloud snapshot exposure) does not yield readable data. Most cloud database services (RDS, Cloud SQL) enable TDE by default with cloud-managed keys, but using customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) provides the key custody control required by regulations like HIPAA and PCI DSS. The performance overhead of TDE on modern hardware is typically less than 5% for I/O-bound workloads.

children.title: Database Audit Log Configuration
children.description: Database audit logs record who accessed what data and when, which is the primary evidence required for compliance frameworks (SOC2, PCI DSS, HIPAA) and forensic investigation after a security incident. PostgreSQL's pgaudit extension and MySQL's audit plugin both provide statement-level and object-level logging that goes beyond the basic query log, capturing the full SQL text, affected objects, and execution context. Audit logs must be shipped to an immutable, external log store immediately—storing them only on the database server means they can be deleted by an attacker who has already compromised the database.

children.title: Centralized Log Management for Compliance
children.description: Shipping database audit logs to a SIEM (Splunk, Elastic Security, Sumo Logic) centralizes security-relevant events across all databases, enabling cross-system correlation that is impossible when logs are silo'd per instance. Compliance frameworks require audit log retention of one to seven years depending on the regulation, which makes log compression and tiered storage (hot for 90 days, warm for 1 year, cold for 7 years) essential for cost management. Alerting on anomalous access patterns in the SIEM—a service account querying a table it has never queried before, or unusually high data export volume—provides real-time threat detection.

children.title: SOC2 Database Controls
children.description: SOC2 Type II certification requires demonstrating that database access controls, encryption, monitoring, and change management processes are consistently applied over a 6-12 month audit period. Common SOC2 database control gaps include missing MFA on database administration access, absent rotation documentation for database credentials, and audit log gaps caused by misconfigured syslog forwarding. Implementing SOC2 controls as infrastructure-as-code with automated compliance testing closes these gaps more reliably than manual checklists.

children.title: Row-Level Security for Multi-Tenant Databases
children.description: Row-level security (RLS) in PostgreSQL enforces data isolation at the database level by filtering query results based on the executing role, ensuring that a tenant can only see their own rows even when application code is buggy or compromised. This provides defense-in-depth beyond application-layer filtering and satisfies audit requirements for multi-tenant SaaS data isolation. The performance overhead of RLS policies is measurable on high-cardinality tables without indexes on the tenant discriminator column, so index design must account for RLS predicates.

db-reliability.title: Chaos Engineering and DR Validation
db-reliability.description: Use chaos engineering to proactively test database resilience under failure conditions. Conduct regular DR drills and measure actual RTO and RPO against targets.
db-reliability.challenge: Design and execute a DR drill for a Patroni PostgreSQL cluster, measure actual RTO and RPO, compare to targets, and produce an improvement plan for any gaps.

children.title: Database-Specific Chaos Experiments
children.description: Generic chaos experiments (kill random pods) test infrastructure resilience but miss database-specific failure modes like replica promotion latency, connection pool drain behavior during failover, and WAL receiver reconnection delays. Designing database-targeted experiments—killing the primary, injecting network latency between primary and replica, filling the transaction log—validates the specific failure paths that database HA systems are designed to handle. Each experiment should have a predefined hypothesis (recovery within 30s), a measurement method (connection error rate), and an abort condition (if >5% errors, stop).

children.title: Chaos Engineering Principles and Safety
children.description: Chaos engineering run without safety controls can turn a controlled experiment into an uncontrolled production incident, which destroys organizational trust in the practice. The key safety controls are: a kill switch to abort the experiment, a minimum steady-state baseline established before injection, and a rollback procedure for each experiment type. Starting with the smallest possible blast radius in lower environments and expanding incrementally to production is the only responsible path for teams new to chaos engineering.

children.title: Gremlin for Controlled Chaos
children.description: Gremlin provides a hosted chaos engineering platform with pre-built attack types for CPU, memory, disk, network, and process failures, with a central control plane that enforces blast radius limits and provides a kill switch. Its host and container agents install on database nodes and can target specific processes (the postgres process, mysqld) rather than terminating entire VMs, which makes experiments more surgical. Gremlin's GameDay feature allows teams to run coordinated multi-system experiments across database, application, and network layers simultaneously.

resources.label: Getting Started with Gremlin for Database Resilience

children.title: Chaos Mesh for Kubernetes Databases
children.description: Chaos Mesh is an open-source Kubernetes-native chaos engineering tool that injects failures into StatefulSet pods running databases, using Kubernetes Custom Resources to define experiments declaratively. Its network chaos features (partition, delay, bandwidth limit) are particularly valuable for testing database replication behavior under degraded network conditions, which is difficult to reproduce with commercial tools that focus on host-level failures. Chaos Mesh integrates with Grafana to overlay experiment timelines on performance dashboards, making experiment impact measurement straightforward.

resources.label: Database Chaos Testing with Chaos Mesh

children.title: Defining and Measuring RTO and RPO
children.description: Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) are business requirements—the maximum acceptable time to restore service and the maximum acceptable data loss—not technical measurements, and they must be agreed with business stakeholders before any DR architecture is designed. Measuring actual RTO and RPO during DR drills reveals whether the architecture meets these requirements and where the gaps are, because theoretical RTO (based on tool benchmarks) routinely differs from operational RTO (which includes human coordination time, DNS propagation, and connection pool warm-up). Documenting the gap between target and actual RTO/RPO and presenting it to leadership creates the organizational support for funding gap-closure work.

resources.label: AWS RTO and RPO Planning Guide
Measuring RTO and RPO in Database DR Drills

children.title: DR Drill Planning and Execution
children.description: A DR drill that is announced, narrated, and supervised is valuable for validation but does not build the organizational muscle memory that an unannounced drill creates. Alternating between announced drills (which allow teams to prepare and measure systematically) and unannounced drills (which test real readiness) provides both data quality and operational authenticity. DR drills for databases specifically must verify three things: that the backup contains the expected data, that the restore completes within RTO, and that the restored database passes application-level integrity checks.

children.title: Resilience Metrics and Scoring
children.description: A resilience score aggregates multiple dimensions of database reliability—backup recency, restore test pass rate, failover drill success rate, SLO compliance percentage, and runbook coverage—into a single dashboard metric that makes reliability progress visible over time. Tracking these scores quarterly reveals whether reliability investments are actually moving the needle or whether teams are spending effort on low-impact work. Scores also provide a forcing function for maintaining reliability work alongside feature development, because a declining score is visible to leadership in a way that anecdotal incident descriptions are not.

children.title: Network Partition Simulation
children.description: Network partitions between a primary database and its replicas are one of the most dangerous failure modes because they can trigger split-brain if the HA system's fencing mechanism is inadequate. Simulating network partitions with tc (traffic control) or Gremlin's network chaos features while observing Patroni's or MHA's response reveals whether the HA system correctly stonith's the old primary and whether application clients experience acceptable error rates during the partition. This test is distinct from a node kill test because the partitioned node is still running and may continue accepting connections that it believes are legitimate.

resources.label: Testing Database Behavior Under Network Partition

redis-engineer.title: Redis Fundamentals
redis-engineer.description: Install Redis and learn the basics of interacting with it using redis-cli. Understand the key-value model, TTL-based expiration, and the most common data types.
redis-engineer.challenge: Use redis-cli to store session data for 100 simulated users with TTL expiry, and write a script that checks how many sessions are still active after 5 minutes.

children.title: Installing Redis Locally
children.description: Getting Redis running locally is the fastest way to build intuition for how it behaves before deploying it anywhere else. A local setup lets you freely experiment with configuration flags, crash and restart the server, and observe exactly how memory and persistence work without operational risk. Understanding the install process also demystifies the binary layout, default config locations, and service management.

resources.label: Install Redis on Linux, macOS, and Windows

children.title: redis-cli Interactive Shell
children.description: redis-cli is the primary tool for interacting with Redis during development, debugging, and ops work. Mastering its flags — like --pipe for bulk loading, --latency for monitoring, and --scan for iterating keys — makes you dramatically more efficient when diagnosing live issues. Every Redis engineer spends significant time in redis-cli, so fluency here directly accelerates everything else you do.

children.title: String GET/SET Operations
children.description: String is Redis's most fundamental type and the one every other pattern builds on. Understanding SET's options — NX, XX, EX, PX, GET — lets you implement atomic compare-and-swap patterns without extra round trips. Because strings can also hold integers and binary blobs, they serve as the foundation for counters, distributed locks, and serialized JSON payloads alike.

children.title: Key Naming Conventions
children.description: Good key naming is one of the most overlooked aspects of Redis design, yet it directly affects observability, memory usage, and operational sanity at scale. Using consistent colon-separated namespaces (e.g., user:1234:session) allows tools like redis-cli's --scan with patterns and keyspace notifications to target logical groups efficiently. Poor naming conventions lead to keyspace collisions, wasted memory, and ops nightmares when you need to purge or migrate specific data.

children.title: Key Expiration with TTL
children.description: TTL-based expiration is Redis's built-in mechanism for automatic memory reclamation and is fundamental to session management, caching, and rate limiting. Without understanding how Redis lazily expires keys (checking on access) and actively sweeps expired keys in the background, you can be surprised by memory not being freed as quickly as expected. Knowing commands like EXPIRE, EXPIREAT, PEXPIRE, and TTL is essential for implementing time-bound data patterns correctly.

children.title: INFO Command Overview
children.description: The INFO command is your window into a live Redis server's health, giving you sections covering memory, CPU, clients, replication state, persistence status, and more in a single call. Learning to read INFO output fluently lets you quickly answer questions like 'is this server hitting memory limits?', 'are replicas in sync?', and 'what's the hit rate on the cache?' without needing external tooling. It's the first command you run when something seems off.

children.title: Redis in Action (Book)
children.description: Redis in Action by Josiah Carlson is the definitive deep-dive text on Redis, covering not just the commands but the patterns and engineering trade-offs behind real-world deployments. It walks through building actual systems — from ad targeting to social networks — so you learn when and why to reach for each data structure rather than just what the commands do. It remains essential reading even years after publication because its core insights about Redis's design philosophy have not changed.

children.title: Redis University RU101
children.description: Redis University's RU101 (Introduction to Redis Data Structures) is the most structured free course available for learning Redis from scratch, built and maintained by the Redis core team. It covers all fundamental data types with interactive exercises and graded quizzes, so you get validated hands-on practice rather than passive reading. Completing it earns a certificate and gives you a solid mental model before moving to advanced topics like clustering or modules.

resources.label: RU101: Introduction to Redis Data Structures

redis-engineer.title: Redis Data Structures
redis-engineer.description: Master all major Redis data structures and understand the use cases each is best suited for. Practice building real-world patterns with lists, sets, hashes, and sorted sets.
redis-engineer.challenge: Build a leaderboard for a gaming app using sorted sets that supports adding scores, retrieving top 10 players, and looking up a specific player's rank.

children.title: Lists as Queues and Stacks
children.description: Redis Lists are doubly-linked lists that enable O(1) push and pop from both ends, making them the natural choice for implementing queues (LPUSH + BRPOP) and stacks (LPUSH + LPOP) in distributed systems. The blocking variants (BLPOP, BRPOP) are especially powerful because they let consumers sleep efficiently until an item arrives, eliminating polling loops. Understanding LRANGE, LTRIM, and LPOS rounds out the list API for use cases ranging from activity feeds to job queues.

resources.label: Redis as a Queue – Practical Tutorial

children.title: Sets for Unique Membership
children.description: Redis Sets are unordered collections of unique strings with O(1) add, remove, and membership testing, making them ideal for tagging systems, friend lists, and deduplication pipelines. Their real power lies in set operations — SUNION, SINTER, SDIFF — which let you compute relationships between groups in a single server-side operation rather than pulling data to the application. SRANDMEMBER and SPOP add random-element access that is useful for sampling and lottery-style features.

children.title: Hashes for Objects
children.description: Redis Hashes map string fields to string values and are the most memory-efficient way to store structured objects in Redis, especially for small records. When an object has fewer than 128 fields and values are short, Redis uses a compact ziplist encoding that can be 10x more memory-efficient than storing each field as a separate key. HSET, HMGET, HINCRBY, and HSCAN give you fine-grained field-level access that avoids deserializing entire objects just to update a single attribute.

children.title: Sorted Sets for Rankings
children.description: Redis Sorted Sets combine the uniqueness of Sets with a floating-point score per member, enabling logarithmic-time ranking operations that would require expensive ORDER BY queries in relational databases. ZADD, ZRANK, ZRANGE, and ZRANGEBYSCORE let you build real-time leaderboards, priority queues, and time-windowed rate limiters with single-digit millisecond response times. The lexicographic range commands (ZRANGEBYLEX) extend sorted sets to index-style lookups over string-sorted members.

resources.label: Building a Leaderboard with Redis Sorted Sets

children.title: Bitmaps and HyperLogLog
children.description: Bitmaps allow you to track binary states for millions of items in kilobytes of memory by using bit offsets as keys — perfect for daily active user flags, feature toggles per user, or attendance tracking across 365 days. HyperLogLog solves a different problem: counting unique visitors or distinct events with a constant 12KB of memory regardless of cardinality, accepting a 0.81% standard error that is acceptable for analytics dashboards. Both structures unlock use cases that would be cost-prohibitive with exact-count approaches at scale.

children.title: Geospatial Commands
children.description: Redis Geo commands (GEOADD, GEODIST, GEORADIUS, GEOSEARCH) store latitude/longitude coordinates using an encoded sorted set and enable radius-based proximity queries with sub-millisecond latency. This makes Redis a compelling choice for location features like 'find stores within 5km' without standing up a separate PostGIS instance or paying for a geo-search service. The encoding uses geohash internally, so spatial precision is bounded but more than sufficient for most consumer-facing proximity use cases.

children.title: Choosing the Right Data Structure
children.description: Redis's speed advantage comes not just from being in-memory but from using the right internal encoding for each use case — and choosing the wrong data structure can make your solution 10x slower or 5x larger in memory. Mapping your domain model to Redis types (rather than forcing everything into strings) is the single biggest architectural decision you make with Redis. Understanding when a Hash beats a String, or when a Sorted Set beats a List, requires both knowing the command APIs and the memory characteristics of each encoding.

resources.label: Redis Data Structures – Redis University RU101

children.title: Internal Encodings and Memory
children.description: Redis automatically switches between compact (ziplist/listpack) and full encodings based on thresholds like hash-max-ziplist-entries, giving you free memory savings when your data is small and automatic promotion when it grows. Knowing these thresholds — and tuning them based on your payload sizes — can reduce memory consumption by 40–60% for hash-heavy workloads. OBJECT ENCODING lets you inspect the active encoding for any key, giving you direct feedback on whether your data shapes are being stored optimally.

redis-engineer.title: Caching Patterns
redis-engineer.description: Implement common caching patterns using Redis to reduce database load and improve application latency. Understand cache invalidation strategies and cache stampede prevention.
redis-engineer.challenge: Add Redis caching to a REST API using cache-aside, implement probabilistic early expiration to prevent stampede, and measure p99 latency before and after.

children.title: Cache-Aside Pattern
children.description: Cache-aside (lazy loading) is the most widely deployed Redis caching pattern because it only loads data into the cache when it is actually requested, keeping the cache lean and avoiding upfront hydration costs. The trade-off is a cold-start penalty on first access and the risk of stale reads if the database changes without a corresponding cache invalidation. Understanding this pattern deeply — including when to use short TTLs vs explicit invalidation — is the foundation of every Redis caching architecture.

children.title: Write-Through Caching
children.description: Write-through ensures the cache is always consistent with the database by writing to both on every mutation, eliminating the stale-read window that cache-aside suffers from. This pattern is most appropriate when read consistency is critical and write latency is acceptable — for example, user profile data that is read far more often than it changes. The cost is that every write pays a double-store penalty, and the cache can accumulate cold data that is never read again if eviction policies are not tuned carefully.

children.title: Cache Invalidation Strategies
children.description: Cache invalidation is notoriously one of the hardest problems in distributed systems because the moment you decouple cache from the source of truth, you create a window where they can diverge. Strategies range from TTL-only (simple but allows controlled staleness) to event-driven invalidation via database triggers or change-data-capture (precise but complex to operate). Choosing the right invalidation model requires understanding your data's write frequency, read sensitivity to staleness, and the operational complexity your team can sustain.

children.title: Cache Stampede Prevention
children.description: A cache stampede (also called thundering herd) occurs when many requests simultaneously discover an expired key and all attempt to regenerate it from the database, creating a sudden spike of expensive queries that can cascade into a full outage. Probabilistic early expiration (PER) is the most elegant defense — it randomly refreshes keys slightly before they expire based on a statistical model, spreading regeneration over time without coordination. Other approaches include locking with SETNX (allowing only one request to regenerate) and request coalescing at the application tier.

children.title: Write-Behind (Write-Back) Caching
children.description: Write-behind caching writes exclusively to Redis and asynchronously flushes changes to the database in batches, making writes feel instant to the application while deferring the more expensive database I/O. This dramatically improves write throughput for high-volume systems but introduces a durability risk: if Redis crashes before the async flush completes, those writes are lost. It is therefore reserved for workloads where some data loss is acceptable, such as analytics counters or activity streams that can tolerate approximations.

resources.label: Redis as Write-Behind Cache – Redis Blog

children.title: Cache Warming Strategies
children.description: Starting with a cold cache after a deployment or failover means your database absorbs full load during the warm-up period, which can cause brownouts in latency-sensitive systems. Cache warming proactively populates the cache before traffic hits, either by replaying recent reads, pre-loading known hot keys, or shadowing production traffic to a new cache before switching. Understanding when and how to warm caches is critical in high-traffic systems where a cold cache can itself trigger the cascade you are trying to prevent.

children.title: Hit Rate and Latency Analysis
children.description: Cache hit rate is the single most important metric for evaluating whether your caching strategy is working — a hit rate below 80% often means your TTLs are too short, your keyspace is too large for available memory, or your eviction policy is wrong. Redis exposes keyspace_hits and keyspace_misses via INFO stats, giving you the raw numbers to compute hit rate in real time. Tracking p99 and p999 latency alongside hit rate tells you the user-visible impact and helps justify cache layer investment to stakeholders.

children.title: Eviction Policies for Caches
children.description: When Redis reaches its maxmemory limit it must evict keys, and the eviction policy you choose determines which keys are removed — allkeys-lru is the most common for general caches, while volatile-lru restricts eviction to keys with TTLs set. Choosing the wrong policy can cause Redis to evict your most frequently accessed keys during memory pressure, collapsing your hit rate exactly when traffic is highest. Tuning maxmemory-policy alongside memory usage monitoring is a critical production operations skill.

redis-engineer.title: Redis Persistence
redis-engineer.description: Understand and configure Redis persistence options including RDB snapshots and Append-Only File. Choose the right persistence mode based on durability requirements.
redis-engineer.challenge: Configure Redis with AOF persistence using everysec fsync, simulate a crash, restart Redis, and verify all committed data is recovered with no loss.

children.title: RDB Snapshot Configuration
children.description: RDB (Redis Database) snapshots produce compact binary files representing a point-in-time state of the dataset, making them ideal for backups and disaster recovery scenarios where small data loss windows (minutes) are acceptable. The SAVE thresholds in redis.conf define when automatic snapshots occur — for example, 'save 900 1' triggers a snapshot if at least one key changed in 15 minutes — and tuning these directly impacts both data safety and I/O load on the server. Understanding BGSAVE vs SAVE (background fork vs blocking save) is essential because a blocking save on a large dataset can stall every client command for seconds.

children.title: BGSAVE and BGREWRITEAOF
children.description: BGSAVE forks the Redis process to write an RDB snapshot in the background while the parent continues serving requests, using copy-on-write semantics so the snapshot reflects the state at fork time without blocking clients. BGREWRITEAOF similarly rewrites the Append-Only File by forking, compacting redundant commands into the minimal set needed to reconstruct the dataset. Both rely on OS-level copy-on-write, so large datasets with high write rates during a background save can cause significant memory amplification — a common production surprise.

children.title: AOF Persistence and fsync Policies
children.description: The Append-Only File logs every write command received by Redis, allowing exact reconstruction of state on restart — at the cost of larger file sizes and slower restart times compared to RDB. The fsync policy controls how often data is flushed to disk: 'always' gives the strongest durability (at most 1 command lost) but adds fsync overhead per write, 'everysec' is the practical sweet spot (at most 1 second of data lost), and 'no' delegates to the OS (fastest but least safe). Getting this trade-off right for your SLA is one of the most important persistence decisions you make.

resources.label: AOF vs RDB: Which Should You Use?

children.title: Mixed Persistence Mode
children.description: Redis's hybrid persistence mode (aof-use-rdb-preamble yes) combines the compact startup speed of RDB with the safety of AOF by embedding an RDB snapshot at the start of the AOF file and appending only subsequent commands. This dramatically reduces restart times for large datasets because Redis loads the RDB preamble in bulk rather than replaying thousands of individual AOF commands. It became the recommended persistence configuration starting with Redis 4.0 and is now the default in Redis 7.

children.title: RDB vs AOF Trade-offs
children.description: Choosing between RDB and AOF (or combining them) requires understanding the exact durability window each provides and the operational characteristics of each approach under load. RDB has fast restarts and compact files but can lose minutes of data on crash, while AOF can lose at most one second of writes but has slower restarts and produces much larger files that must be periodically rewritten. Most production Redis deployments use both — AOF for operational durability and RDB for faster disaster recovery restores.

resources.label: Choosing Redis Persistence Strategy – Redis Blog

children.title: Persistence and Performance Impact
children.description: Persistence adds I/O overhead that can materially impact Redis throughput and latency, particularly on write-heavy workloads where fsync costs dominate or frequent background saves compete for I/O bandwidth. Understanding how to benchmark persistence overhead using redis-benchmark — and how to detect it in production via latency monitoring — lets you tune the persistence configuration to meet your durability requirements without unnecessarily sacrificing performance. On cloud instances with network-attached storage, the I/O characteristics are fundamentally different from local NVMe, so always test in your actual environment.

children.title: Redis Backup and Restore Procedures
children.description: A Redis backup strategy is only as good as your last tested restore — many teams discover their backup process is broken only when they need it during an incident. RDB files can be restored simply by copying them into place before Redis starts, while AOF files can be validated with redis-check-aof and replayed on startup. Automating backups with versioned storage in S3 or equivalent, and periodically running restore drills, is the difference between a recoverable incident and a data loss event.

children.title: Redis Crash Recovery Validation
children.description: Validating crash recovery is not optional — you need to prove that your persistence configuration actually meets your durability SLA before a real incident forces you to find out. The redis-check-rdb and redis-check-aof tools can detect corruption in persistence files, which occasionally occurs after unexpected shutdowns on systems that do not guarantee fsync order. Building a runbook that covers crash detection, file integrity checks, and step-by-step recovery procedures is an essential part of operating Redis in production.

redis-engineer.title: Pub/Sub and Redis Streams
redis-engineer.description: Build real-time messaging and event processing systems using Redis Pub/Sub and Redis Streams. Use consumer groups for reliable at-least-once message processing.
redis-engineer.challenge: Build an order processing pipeline using Redis Streams where producers emit orders and two consumer groups (fulfillment and analytics) process them independently.

children.title: Pub/Sub Publish and Subscribe
children.description: Redis Pub/Sub provides a fire-and-forget broadcast mechanism where publishers send messages to channels and all currently connected subscribers receive them, enabling lightweight real-time notification systems. Its simplicity makes it ideal for use cases like live dashboards, chat applications, and cache invalidation signals between application servers. The critical constraint is that messages are not persisted — a subscriber that disconnects even briefly will miss all messages sent during its absence, which is why Pub/Sub alone is unsuitable for reliable event processing.

children.title: Pub/Sub Limitations and When to Move On
children.description: Understanding Redis Pub/Sub's limitations is as important as knowing how to use it — no message persistence, no consumer groups, no acknowledgments, and no backpressure means that slow or offline subscribers silently lose data. These limitations drive most serious event processing workloads to Redis Streams, which was designed specifically to address them. Knowing when Pub/Sub is sufficient (ephemeral signals) versus when Streams are necessary (durable, acknowledgeable event logs) is a key architectural judgment for any Redis engineer.

resources.label: When to Use Redis Pub/Sub vs Streams – Redis Blog

children.title: Redis Streams XADD and XREAD
children.description: Redis Streams are an append-only log data structure where XADD writes entries with auto-generated or custom IDs and XREAD retrieves entries starting from a given position, providing durable, ordered event storage within Redis. Unlike Pub/Sub, entries in a Stream persist until explicitly trimmed, allowing consumers that temporarily disconnect to catch up by resuming from their last-seen ID. Understanding the ID format (millisecond-sequence) and how XREAD blocking works is essential before building any stream-based pipeline.

children.title: Stream Trimming Strategies
children.description: Without trimming, Redis Streams grow indefinitely and will eventually consume all available memory — trimming is the mechanism that prevents this by capping the stream either by length (MAXLEN) or by minimum ID (MINID). XADD supports inline trimming via the MAXLEN option, and the ~ (approximate) modifier lets Redis trim less frequently for much better write throughput while still staying close to the target size. Understanding how trimming interacts with consumer group pending entry lists (PEL) is important — trimmed entries that have not been acknowledged can create orphaned entries in the PEL.

resources.label: Managing Redis Stream Size – Redis Blog

children.title: Consumer Groups with XREADGROUP
children.description: Consumer groups enable multiple competing consumers to divide stream entries among themselves, with Redis tracking which entries each consumer has received and which have been acknowledged, giving you at-least-once delivery semantics. XREADGROUP assigns entries to a specific consumer within the group and moves them into a pending entry list (PEL) until the consumer calls XACK, at which point Redis knows the entry was processed successfully. This model is analogous to Kafka consumer groups and enables scalable, fault-tolerant stream processing entirely within Redis without external coordination.

children.title: Pending Message Acknowledgment
children.description: The pending entry list (PEL) tracks all messages delivered to a consumer group that have not yet been acknowledged, and XPENDING lets you inspect it to find messages that may be stuck due to consumer crashes or processing failures. XCLAIM lets you reassign a stuck message to another consumer after a configurable idle timeout, enabling automatic recovery without manual intervention. Building proper dead-letter handling for messages that exceed a maximum delivery count is the final piece of a robust consumer group pipeline.

children.title: Event Sourcing with Redis Streams
children.description: Redis Streams are a natural fit for event sourcing within a bounded context — each domain event is appended with XADD, and the stream itself becomes the authoritative log from which current state can be derived. This enables audit trails, temporal queries ('what was the state at time T?'), and replay-based debugging that are very difficult to build with traditional CRUD data stores. The trade-off versus a dedicated event store like EventStoreDB is that Redis Streams do not support long-term retention at massive scale without careful trimming and external archival.

resources.label: Event Sourcing with Redis Streams – Redis Blog
Redis Streams as an Event Store

children.title: Redis Streams vs Kafka
children.description: Redis Streams and Apache Kafka solve the same fundamental problem — durable, ordered, consumer-group-based event streaming — but at very different scales and operational footprints. Redis Streams win on simplicity (no ZooKeeper, no broker cluster, no topic partitioning configuration) and latency (sub-millisecond), while Kafka wins on throughput (millions of events per second), retention (terabytes of data), and ecosystem maturity. Understanding where each fits in your architecture prevents both the mistake of using Kafka for simple internal events and Redis Streams for petabyte-scale pipelines.

resources.label: Redis Streams vs Apache Kafka – Comparison
When to Use Redis Streams vs Kafka – YouTube

redis-engineer.title: Redis Security and Access Control
redis-engineer.description: Secure Redis with ACL-based user management, TLS for encrypted connections, and network-level restrictions. Audit Redis access and apply least-privilege principles.
redis-engineer.challenge: Create Redis ACL users with read-only, read-write, and admin roles, enable TLS, and verify that each user can only perform the commands matching their role.

children.title: Redis ACL User Creation
children.description: Redis ACLs (Access Control Lists), introduced in Redis 6, replace the legacy single-password requirepass model with proper user-level authentication that supports per-user passwords, enabled/disabled state, and fine-grained permission assignments. Creating dedicated service users with ACL SETUSER — one for read-only replicas, one for cache writers, one for admin operations — enforces least-privilege and limits the blast radius of a compromised credential. ACL LOG lets you monitor access violations in real time, giving you an audit trail that requirepass never provided.

children.title: ACL Command and Key Permissions
children.description: Redis ACLs let you control not just which commands a user can run but which keyspace they can access, using pattern-based key selectors (e.g., ~session:* to allow access only to session-prefixed keys). This enables true multi-tenancy within a single Redis instance — a powerful operational pattern that reduces infrastructure costs by consolidating multiple logical databases onto one server without data isolation risk. Understanding the command permission categories (like +@read or -@dangerous) lets you grant broad capability classes rather than enumerating individual commands.

children.title: TLS Configuration for Redis
children.description: Enabling TLS on Redis encrypts all data in transit between clients and the server, preventing credential and data interception on shared networks or cloud environments where traffic traverses virtual switches you do not fully control. Redis 6 added native TLS support via the tls-port, tls-cert-file, tls-key-file, and tls-ca-cert-file configuration directives, with mutual TLS (mTLS) available for environments that require client certificate authentication. Certificate rotation procedures and the impact of TLS on connection handshake latency are important operational considerations.

children.title: Protected Mode and Network Restrictions
children.description: Redis's protected mode prevents it from accepting external connections when no authentication is configured, acting as a safety net against the alarming number of Redis instances that have been publicly exposed to the internet and subsequently compromised. Beyond protected mode, binding Redis to specific interfaces (bind 127.0.0.1) and firewalling port 6379 at the network level provides defense-in-depth that does not rely on application-level controls alone. Understanding why Redis was historically exploited for cryptocurrency mining and data theft emphasizes why these network controls must be verified, not assumed.

children.title: ACL Audit Logging
children.description: ACL LOG records denied commands and authentication failures in an in-memory ring buffer, giving you visibility into unauthorized access attempts without needing an external SIEM for basic forensics. Regularly reviewing ACL LOG in production catches misconfigured clients, overly broad permission grants that are never used, and external probe attempts early. Coupling ACL LOG with application-level error rate monitoring creates a layered alerting strategy where permission denials are surfaced as both a security signal and an operational incident.

children.title: Disabling Dangerous Commands
children.description: Commands like FLUSHALL, CONFIG, DEBUG, and KEYS can be catastrophic in production — FLUSHALL deletes the entire dataset instantly, KEYS blocks the server while scanning the full keyspace, and CONFIG REWRITE can silently corrupt your configuration. Redis allows you to rename or disable specific commands in redis.conf using the rename-command directive, effectively removing them from the API surface for all users including admins connecting without an elevated ACL role. This is a critical hardening step for shared Redis instances and for protecting against accidental operator errors.

children.title: Redis Security in Cloud Environments
children.description: Running Redis on AWS, GCP, or Azure introduces cloud-specific security considerations like VPC placement, security group rules, IAM-based authentication (for managed services like ElastiCache), and encryption at rest managed by the cloud provider's KMS. Managed Redis services often abstract away some security configuration but introduce their own authorization models that differ from native Redis ACLs. Understanding the shared responsibility model for your specific cloud Redis deployment prevents gaps between what you think is secured and what actually is.

children.title: requirepass vs ACLs Migration
children.description: Many legacy Redis deployments still use the single requirepass directive, which provides only a shared password with no per-user control, no key-level restrictions, and no audit log. Migrating from requirepass to a full ACL model requires creating users that match your existing application roles, testing them in staging, and carefully coordinating the cutover across all clients simultaneously to avoid authentication failures. Understanding the backwards compatibility path — requirepass and ACLs can coexist during migration — makes this transition much safer.

resources.label: Redis 6 ACL Migration Guide – Redis Blog

redis-engineer.title: Redis Replication and Sentinel
redis-engineer.description: Configure Redis replication for data redundancy and set up Redis Sentinel for automatic failover and service discovery. Monitor replication health.
redis-engineer.challenge: Deploy a Redis primary with two replicas and a 3-sentinel setup, kill the primary, and verify Sentinel promotes a replica and clients reconnect automatically.

children.title: Primary-Replica Replication
children.description: Redis replication creates read replicas that asynchronously receive every write applied to the primary, providing both data redundancy and the ability to scale read-heavy workloads across multiple nodes. The initial synchronization uses an RDB snapshot (full sync) while subsequent replication uses an in-memory replication backlog (partial resync), which means reconnecting replicas that fell behind by too much will trigger a costly full resync. Understanding replication lag, the replication backlog size (repl-backlog-size), and replica read-only mode are essential for operating replicated setups safely.

children.title: Replication Configuration
children.description: Configuring replication requires setting 'replicaof <primary-ip> <port>' on each replica, and optionally configuring replica-lazy-flush, replica-read-only, and min-replicas-to-write for semi-synchronous durability guarantees. The min-replicas-to-write and min-replicas-max-lag settings create a write-safety mode where the primary refuses writes unless a minimum number of replicas are in sync, preventing data loss scenarios where a primary takes writes that replicas never received before a crash. Choosing the right replication topology (one primary with two replicas vs a chained replication tree) requires understanding the trade-offs in replication fan-out and latency.

children.title: Redis Sentinel Setup and Quorum
children.description: Redis Sentinel is a distributed system of Sentinel processes that monitor Redis primaries and replicas, coordinate automatic failover when the primary becomes unavailable, and provide service discovery so clients can find the current primary without manual reconfiguration. The quorum is the minimum number of Sentinels that must agree a primary is down before initiating failover — setting it too low risks false failovers from network partitions, while setting it too high means a minority Sentinel failure can block legitimate failovers. A minimum of 3 Sentinels is the recommended deployment for any production setup to ensure quorum can always be reached.

children.title: Automatic Failover Process
children.description: When Sentinel determines the primary is down, it orchestrates an election to select the best replica for promotion based on replication offset (most up-to-date data) and replica priority settings. Understanding the exact sequence — ODOWN declaration, Sentinel leader election, replica promotion, old primary reconfiguration as new replica — lets you predict failover behavior and tune timeouts appropriately for your network and latency requirements. Failover time is determined by the down-after-milliseconds and failover-timeout Sentinel parameters, and tuning them requires balancing fast recovery against false-positive sensitivity.

children.title: Connecting Clients Through Sentinel
children.description: Clients that are Sentinel-aware connect to Sentinel first to discover the current primary's address, then maintain that connection until they receive a failover notification and re-query Sentinel for the new primary. Libraries like ioredis (Node.js), Jedis (Java), and redis-py (Python) all support Sentinel mode natively — you configure a list of Sentinel endpoints rather than a single Redis address, and the client handles topology changes transparently. Testing your client's behavior during an actual failover in staging is the only way to confirm that reconnection logic works as expected under your application's specific request patterns.

children.title: Monitoring Replication Health
children.description: Replication health monitoring is a first-class operational concern — a lagged or disconnected replica is not a problem until your primary fails, at which point a stale replica being promoted can cause significant data loss or application errors. The INFO replication section exposes the master_repl_offset, replica_repl_offset, and lag in seconds for every connected replica, making it straightforward to alert when replication lag exceeds your acceptable window. Combining this with Prometheus redis_exporter metrics gives you historical trend data to catch gradual replication degradation before it becomes a crisis.

children.title: Redis HA Architecture Design
children.description: A proper Redis HA architecture requires decisions across multiple dimensions — number of replicas, number of Sentinels, placement across availability zones, network topology, and client reconnection behavior. Placing primary, replicas, and Sentinels across at least two availability zones while ensuring Sentinel quorum can be reached even during a single AZ outage requires careful planning. Understanding the failure modes — including the split-brain scenario where Sentinels in different network partitions both promote a replica — is essential before signing off on a HA design.

resources.label: Redis HA with Sentinel – Full Setup Guide

children.title: Replica Reads for Scaling
children.description: Routing read-only traffic to replicas is a powerful horizontal scaling technique for read-heavy workloads, but it requires application-level awareness of replication lag — reads from a replica may return data that is slightly behind the primary. Frameworks like ioredis support replica read routing via the 'scaleReads' option, making the routing transparent at the client level. Setting replica-read-only yes (the default) ensures replicas never accidentally accept writes that would diverge from the primary and get overwritten on replication sync.

redis-engineer.title: Redis Cluster
redis-engineer.description: Deploy and manage a Redis Cluster for horizontal scaling across multiple nodes. Understand hash slot distribution, resharding, and how clients interact with cluster topology.
redis-engineer.challenge: Set up a 6-node Redis Cluster (3 primary, 3 replica), distribute a keyspace across it, simulate a primary node failure, and verify automatic failover.

children.title: Hash Slot Assignment and Data Distribution
children.description: Redis Cluster divides the keyspace into 16,384 hash slots and assigns contiguous ranges of slots to each primary node, so every key deterministically maps to a slot via CRC16(key) % 16384 and therefore to a specific node. Understanding this model is fundamental because it explains why multi-key operations only work when all keys hash to the same slot, and why hash tags ({user}.* keys share the same slot) are the mechanism to colocate related keys. Uneven key distribution — where certain slots hold disproportionate data — is a real cluster operational problem that requires resharding to fix.

resources.label: Redis Hash Tags for Key Colocation

children.title: MOVED and ASK Redirects
children.description: When a cluster client sends a command to a node that does not own the target key's slot, the node returns a MOVED redirect with the correct node's address rather than proxying the request — this is fundamental to how cluster topology is communicated to clients. ASK redirects occur during resharding when a slot is partially migrated and a key has already moved to the target node — the client must send an ASKING command to the new node before retrying. Cluster-aware clients like ioredis and Jedis handle MOVED/ASK automatically by maintaining a slot-to-node mapping cache, but understanding the underlying mechanism is critical for debugging unexpected redirect storms.

resources.label: Redis Cluster MOVED and ASK Redirects

children.title: Cluster Setup with redis-cli
children.description: The 'redis-cli --cluster create' command bootstraps a new cluster from a set of standalone Redis nodes, automatically assigning hash slot ranges and configuring the cluster topology — making cluster creation remarkably simple for what is conceptually a complex distributed system. Understanding the node announcement addresses, the cluster-node-timeout setting, and the meet/join sequence that establishes the gossip protocol mesh is important for setting up clusters in environments with non-trivial networking like Kubernetes or private cloud VPCs. Running CLUSTER INFO and CLUSTER NODES after setup verifies that all nodes see each other and that slot coverage is complete.

resources.label: Redis Cluster Setup Step by Step – YouTube

children.title: Resharding and Rebalancing
children.description: Resharding moves hash slots between nodes to add capacity or balance load, and 'redis-cli --cluster reshard' automates the process of migrating keys slot by slot using MIGRATE commands while the cluster continues serving traffic. Understanding that resharding is non-atomic — traffic is served throughout via ASK redirects — means you must test client behavior under resharding conditions before doing it in production for the first time. 'redis-cli --cluster rebalance' automates slot distribution to equalize load across nodes, which is essential after adding new nodes to an existing cluster.

children.title: Node Failure and Automatic Failover
children.description: Redis Cluster uses the gossip protocol (cluster bus on port 6379+10000) for nodes to exchange state and detect failures — a node is marked PFAIL (possible fail) by a peer that cannot reach it, and promoted to FAIL when enough peers agree within the cluster-node-timeout window. When a primary fails, its replicas hold an election and the one with the most up-to-date replication offset wins, taking over the primary's slots within seconds. If a primary fails and all its replicas also fail (or do not exist), that portion of the keyspace becomes unavailable — Redis Cluster does not handle this scenario, which is why replica deployment is not optional.

children.title: Cluster Bus Protocol
children.description: The cluster bus is a separate TCP connection mesh on port 6379+10000 that Redis nodes use for gossip — exchanging cluster state, failure detection votes, and epoch counters that provide consistency guarantees for leadership elections. Each node maintains a full view of cluster topology and gossips with a random subset of peers on each heartbeat cycle, achieving eventual consistency across the cluster without a central coordinator. Understanding the cluster bus is important for firewall rules (the bus port must be accessible between all nodes), for diagnosing split-brain scenarios, and for understanding why cluster nodes must have stable addresses.

children.title: Cluster-Aware Client Libraries
children.description: Cluster-aware clients must maintain a slot-to-node mapping cache, handle MOVED/ASK redirects, and reconnect to the new primary after a failover — all behaviors that standard Redis clients do not implement and which will cause failures if you connect a non-cluster client to a cluster. Libraries like ioredis (Node.js), redis-py with RedisCluster, and Jedis ClusterMode all support these requirements, but each has different configuration options for pipeline support, connection pooling, and retry behavior under resharding. Testing client behavior during a live failover in staging is mandatory before relying on a cluster client in production.

children.title: Redis Cluster Limitations
children.description: Redis Cluster has several important limitations that affect application design: multi-key operations (MSET, MGET, SUNION, etc.) only work when all keys are in the same slot, Lua scripts can only access keys in a single slot, and database selection (SELECT) is not supported (only DB 0 exists). Hash tags are the workaround for multi-key operations — by wrapping a common component in braces ({user:123}.profile and {user:123}.settings hash to the same slot — but this can cause hotspot slots if the tag distribution is uneven. Knowing these limitations upfront shapes your key design decisions from the beginning.

redis-engineer.title: Redis Modules and Advanced Features
redis-engineer.description: Extend Redis functionality using official modules for full-text search, JSON storage, time-series data, and probabilistic data structures. Write Lua scripts for atomic operations.
redis-engineer.challenge: Build a product catalog using RedisJSON for storage and RedisSearch for full-text and faceted search, serving sub-millisecond query responses.

children.title: RedisJSON Document Storage
children.description: RedisJSON enables native JSON document storage in Redis with O(1) path-based access using JSONPath, eliminating the round-trip of deserializing an entire blob just to read or update a nested field. This is transformative for document-oriented workloads that previously had to serialize everything to a string — partial updates with JSON.SET on a subpath are both faster and more memory-efficient than replacing the entire serialized blob. RedisJSON integrates with RedisSearch to enable indexing and querying over JSON documents, creating a full document database experience within Redis.

children.title: RedisSearch Full-Text Indexing
children.description: RedisSearch provides full-text search, numeric range queries, geo queries, and faceted filtering over Redis data with sub-millisecond query latency, powered by inverted indexes maintained by the module itself. Defining a search schema with FT.CREATE specifies which fields to index (text, numeric, geo, tag) and how to tokenize them, after which FT.SEARCH gives you Elasticsearch-style queries without the operational overhead of a separate search cluster. The integration with RedisJSON means you can search over JSON documents natively, making Redis a compelling replacement for lightweight Elasticsearch deployments.

children.title: Lua Scripting for Atomicity
children.description: Lua scripts executed via EVAL run atomically on the Redis server — no other command can interleave during script execution — enabling complex read-modify-write sequences that would otherwise require optimistic locking with WATCH/MULTI/EXEC. This is the correct way to implement patterns like 'increment a counter only if it is below a threshold' or 'transfer a value from one key to another without race conditions'. The constraint is that Lua scripts block all other operations during execution, so scripts must be fast; anything over a few milliseconds should be redesigned as a pipeline or a different data structure approach.

children.title: EVAL, EVALSHA, and Script Caching
children.description: EVAL sends the full Lua script text with every call, which wastes bandwidth for large scripts called at high frequency — EVALSHA lets you cache a script on the server using SCRIPT LOAD and then call it by its SHA1 hash, eliminating repeated transmission. Script caching is per-server and lost on restart (or on SCRIPT FLUSH), so applications must handle NOSCRIPT errors by falling back to EVAL and reloading. Redis 7 introduced Redis Functions as a more robust alternative to Lua scripts, providing persistent server-side storage and better library organization.

resources.label: Redis Functions – The Next Generation of Scripting

children.title: RedisTimeSeries for Metrics
children.description: RedisTimeSeries provides purpose-built time-series storage with automatic downsampling via configurable retention rules, compaction aggregations (sum, avg, min, max, count), and labeling for multi-dimensional queries — all at Redis's characteristic sub-millisecond latency. It eliminates the need for a separate InfluxDB or Prometheus-style storage tier for application metrics that need to be stored and queried in real time. TS.ADD, TS.RANGE, and TS.MRANGE are the core commands, and the integration with Grafana via the Redis datasource plugin makes dashboarding straightforward.

children.title: Bloom Filter Module
children.description: The RedisBloom module implements probabilistic data structures — Bloom filters, Cuckoo filters, Count-Min Sketches, and Top-K — that can answer membership and frequency questions with fixed memory regardless of dataset size. A Bloom filter's BF.EXISTS check tells you 'this element is definitely not in the set' or 'this element is probably in the set', making it perfect for database cache prefiltering (avoid querying the database for keys you know do not exist) or spam/duplicate detection. The false positive rate is configurable at creation time, letting you tune the memory/accuracy trade-off for your specific use case.

resources.label: Bloom Filters with Redis – Redis University

children.title: Redis Stack and Module Deployment
children.description: Redis Stack bundles RedisJSON, RedisSearch, RedisGraph, RedisTimeSeries, and RedisBloom into a single distribution, eliminating the complexity of loading individual modules via the loadmodule directive. Deploying Redis Stack simplifies operations significantly for teams that need multiple modules, but it also means all module versions are tied to the Stack release cadence rather than independently upgradeable. Understanding the module loading mechanism (loadmodule /path/to/module.so in redis.conf) and module API compatibility constraints is important when building custom modules or when operating environments where only select modules are approved.

children.title: Redis Functions (Redis 7+)
children.description: Redis Functions, introduced in Redis 7, replace ad-hoc Lua scripts with a proper library system where function code is stored persistently on the server, survives restarts, and can be managed with versioning and documentation. Unlike EVAL scripts which are one-off anonymous functions, Redis Functions are named, organized into libraries, and replicated to cluster nodes and replicas automatically. This makes them suitable for production-grade server-side business logic that previously required either application-level coordination or fragile EVALSHA patterns.

redis-engineer.title: Production Redis Operations
redis-engineer.description: Operate Redis in production by tuning memory, eviction policies, and connection settings. Monitor with Prometheus and run capacity planning using redis-benchmark.
redis-engineer.challenge: Run redis-benchmark to profile a Redis Cluster under load, tune maxmemory with allkeys-lru eviction, and build a Grafana dashboard tracking eviction rate and memory fragmentation ratio.

children.title: maxmemory and Eviction Policies
children.description: Setting maxmemory caps Redis's memory usage and triggers the configured eviction policy when the limit is reached — failing to set this on a caching workload means Redis will consume all available server memory until the OS OOM-kills the process. The allkeys-lru policy (evict the least recently used key across all keys) is the right default for most caches, while volatile-lru (only evict keys with TTLs) is appropriate when Redis holds both cached data and persistent state that must never be evicted. Monitoring the evicted_keys metric and the mem_fragmentation_ratio in INFO memory gives you continuous feedback on whether your eviction configuration is working as intended.

children.title: Memory Optimization with Object Encoding
children.description: Redis automatically uses compact encodings (ziplist/listpack for small hashes, sets, and sorted sets; intset for integer-only sets) that can be dramatically more memory-efficient than their full counterparts, and tuning the thresholds that trigger promotion to full encodings is one of the highest-ROI memory optimizations available. OBJECT ENCODING lets you inspect what encoding any key is using, and OBJECT IDLETIME and OBJECT FREQ help identify cold keys for manual eviction or archival. Running redis-cli --bigkeys and --memkeys identifies the largest consumers in your keyspace, giving you targeted objects to optimize rather than blind global tuning.

resources.label: Redis Memory Usage Analysis Techniques – YouTube

children.title: Connection Pooling Best Practices
children.description: Redis is single-threaded for command execution, which means maintaining thousands of idle persistent connections consumes memory (each connection costs roughly 20KB) and TCP state without providing any throughput benefit. Using a connection pool with a bounded maximum size — sized to the actual parallelism of your application threads — reduces connection overhead while ensuring commands are never queued behind idle connections. Most production Redis clients configure a pool of 10–50 connections per application instance, and the right number depends on your concurrency model and median command latency.

children.title: Pipelining for Throughput
children.description: Redis pipelining sends multiple commands in a single TCP round trip without waiting for each response, dramatically increasing throughput for workloads that need to issue many sequential commands — benchmark tests routinely show 5–10x throughput improvement with pipelining enabled. Unlike transactions (MULTI/EXEC), pipelining does not guarantee atomicity but also does not block the server between commands, making it suitable for bulk operations where order matters but strict atomicity does not. Understanding the trade-off between pipeline batch size and memory pressure (the server buffers all responses until the pipeline flushes) helps you choose the right batch size for your workload.

children.title: Prometheus redis_exporter
children.description: The oliver006/redis_exporter scrapes Redis's INFO output and exposes it as Prometheus metrics, giving you time-series data on memory usage, hit rates, connection counts, replication lag, and command latency histograms that can be alerted on and trended. Deploying the exporter as a sidecar container alongside each Redis instance is the standard Kubernetes pattern, and the included Grafana dashboard templates give you production-ready visualization in minutes. Custom metrics via the redis_exporter's check_single_keys and check_key_groups parameters let you track business-specific values (e.g., queue depth from a List LLEN) as Prometheus metrics without code changes.

children.title: Latency Monitoring and History
children.description: Redis's built-in latency monitoring (latency-monitor-threshold in redis.conf) records individual command executions that exceed a configured threshold in microseconds, and LATENCY HISTORY / LATENCY LATEST let you query these events to pinpoint the exact operations causing outlier latency. This is invaluable for diagnosing p99/p999 latency spikes that do not appear in averages — a BGSAVE fork, a slow Lua script, or a large key expiration can cause a single command to take 100ms while the mean stays under 1ms. Coupling Redis latency monitoring with application-level distributed tracing gives you the full picture from user request to Redis command.

children.title: redis-benchmark for Capacity Planning
children.description: redis-benchmark is the built-in load testing tool that measures how many operations per second your Redis instance can handle under various pipeline depths, concurrency levels, and data sizes — giving you the baseline numbers needed for capacity planning and hardware sizing decisions. Running redis-benchmark with --cluster, -P (pipeline), -c (connections), and -n (total requests) against your exact production hardware configuration tells you your true throughput ceiling before you hit it under real load. The results are sensitive to network round-trip time, key size, and whether persistence is enabled, so always benchmark in conditions that match your production environment.

children.title: Grafana Dashboard for Redis
children.description: A well-designed Grafana dashboard for Redis exposes the four golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation) alongside Redis-specific indicators like eviction rate, memory fragmentation ratio, keyspace hit rate, and replication lag — giving operators a single pane of glass for assessing Redis health at a glance. The Grafana Labs Redis dashboard (Dashboard ID 11835) is a battle-tested starting point that visualizes all critical redis_exporter metrics out of the box. Building alert rules on top of Grafana — alerting when hit rate drops below 85%, when memory approaches maxmemory, or when replication lag exceeds 5 seconds — turns the dashboard from passive monitoring into proactive incident prevention.

resources.label: Redis Production Monitoring with Grafana – YouTube

cassandra-engineer.title: Cassandra Architecture Fundamentals
cassandra-engineer.description: Learn Cassandra's peer-to-peer distributed architecture and how it achieves high availability without a single point of failure. Install a local cluster and use cqlsh for basic operations.
cassandra-engineer.challenge: Set up a 3-node Cassandra cluster locally, verify ring status with nodetool, create a keyspace, and insert and query data using cqlsh.

children.title: Peer-to-Peer Ring Architecture
children.description: Cassandra uses a masterless ring topology where every node is equal, eliminating single points of failure that plague traditional primary-replica setups. Understanding the ring model is essential because it dictates how data is distributed, how tokens are assigned, and how the cluster responds to node failures. Engineers who internalize this model can predict data locality and design schemas that exploit it.

children.title: Consistent Hashing and Token Assignment
children.description: Consistent hashing is the algorithm Cassandra uses to map partition keys to nodes, ensuring minimal data movement when the ring grows or shrinks. Without understanding consistent hashing, you cannot reason about data distribution, load balancing, or the behavior of virtual nodes (vnodes). Vnodes in particular allow fine-grained token ownership that smooths rebalancing when nodes join or leave.

children.title: Gossip-Based Cluster State
children.description: Cassandra nodes discover each other and track cluster state through a gossip protocol inspired by epidemic algorithms, propagating node metadata without a central coordinator. This mechanism is why Cassandra achieves near-instantaneous topology awareness without a leader — every node maintains an eventually consistent view of the ring. Understanding gossip helps you diagnose split-brain scenarios and tune gossip convergence for large clusters.

children.title: Phi Accrual Failure Detector
children.description: Cassandra uses the Phi Accrual Failure Detector to decide whether a node is down, providing a continuous suspicion score rather than a binary alive/dead verdict. This adaptive approach reduces false positives in high-latency environments and is critical for preventing unnecessary cluster restructuring during network hiccups. Knowing how to tune phi_convict_threshold is essential for operating clusters in cloud environments with variable network conditions.

children.title: Installing and Running Cassandra Locally
children.description: Getting a local Cassandra node or cluster running is the fastest way to build intuition about the system before working with production deployments. Docker-based setups let you spin up multi-node clusters on a laptop and observe gossip, token allocation, and ring status in real time with nodetool. Hands-on practice with cqlsh for basic DDL and DML sets the foundation for all later data modeling and operations work.

children.title: nodetool: The Cluster Management CLI
children.description: nodetool is the primary operational interface for Cassandra clusters, exposing commands for ring inspection, repair scheduling, compaction control, and performance stats. Mastering nodetool status, info, tpstats, and cfstats gives you visibility into cluster health that no external monitoring tool can fully replicate. Every on-call engineer working with Cassandra must be fluent with nodetool to respond to incidents effectively.

children.title: Cassandra: The Definitive Guide
children.description: This O'Reilly book by Jeff Carpenter and Eben Hewson is the most comprehensive reference for Cassandra engineers, covering architecture internals, data modeling, and operations with production-depth detail. Reading it alongside the official documentation fills gaps that blog posts and tutorials leave, especially around storage engine internals and multi-datacenter topologies. Engineers who absorb this book develop a mental model that allows them to reason through novel problems rather than just copy-pasting solutions.

children.title: DataStax Academy Free Training
children.description: DataStax Academy provides free self-paced courses on Cassandra architecture, CQL, data modeling, and operations that are taught by engineers with deep production experience. These courses use interactive exercises and quizzes to reinforce concepts, making them more effective than passive reading for building retention. Completing DS201 and DS220 from DataStax Academy establishes a solid foundation before tackling production deployments.

cassandra-engineer.title: CQL and Data Types
cassandra-engineer.description: Learn the Cassandra Query Language in depth including all data types, collection types, user-defined types, and counter tables.
topics: CQL data types (text, uuid, timeuuid, etc.)
cassandra-engineer.challenge: Design a user profile table using UDTs for addresses and collections for tags, insert sample data, and demonstrate updating individual collection elements.

children.title: UUID and TIMEUUID for Unique Identifiers
children.description: UUIDs are the preferred primary key type in Cassandra because they generate globally unique identifiers without coordination, perfectly suited for a distributed system where no single node can issue monotonic IDs. TIMEUUID (version 1 UUID) embeds a timestamp, making it invaluable for time-ordered data like event logs and message feeds without requiring a separate timestamp column. Misusing plain auto-increment integers in Cassandra causes hotspots because sequential IDs route all writes to a single token range.

resources.label: Cassandra Primary Key Design with UUIDs

children.title: Numeric, Text, and Blob Types
children.description: CQL's type system distinguishes between int, bigint, varint, decimal, and double — choosing the wrong numeric type leads to silent precision loss or unnecessary storage overhead at scale. The text type (UTF-8 string) and blob type (raw bytes) serve different purposes: text is for human-readable content while blob handles binary payloads like serialized protobuf messages. Understanding CQL type semantics helps you build schemas that are both correct and storage-efficient across billions of rows.

children.title: List, Set, and Map Collections
children.description: CQL collections (list, set, map) allow storing multiple values in a single cell, useful for tags, preferences, or metadata without requiring a separate table. However, collections are read and written atomically as a whole when using older protocols, meaning updating a single element requires careful use of element-level update syntax to avoid performance pitfalls with large collections. Knowing when to use a set versus a list versus a separate table is a critical data modeling decision that affects both read performance and tombstone accumulation.

children.title: User-Defined Types (UDTs)
children.description: UDTs let you define named composite types that can be embedded into table columns, enabling structured sub-objects like addresses, coordinates, or configuration objects without flattening them into multiple columns. They improve schema readability and allow schema evolution through adding new fields to the UDT without altering the parent table. UDTs are stored inline within the row, so they share the partition's tombstone and compaction behavior — making them ideal for small, stable nested structures.

children.title: Counter Tables
